**Omaha**[[a]](#cite_note-8) is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Nebraska "List of cities in Nebraska") in the U.S. state of [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"). It is located in the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") along the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"), about 10 mi (15 km) north of the mouth of the [Platte River](/wiki/Platte_River "Platte River"). Omaha had a population of 486,051 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), making it the [41st-most populous U.S. city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population").[[8]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-9) The eight-county [Omaha–Council Bluffs metropolitan area](/wiki/Omaha%E2%80%93Council_Bluffs_metropolitan_area "Omaha–Council Bluffs metropolitan area") extending into [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") has approximately 1 million residents, the [55th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area#United_States "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S.[[5]](#cite_note-2024Pop-5) It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Douglas County](/wiki/Douglas_County,_Nebraska "Douglas County, Nebraska").[[9]](#cite_note-GR6-10)

Omaha's pioneer period began in 1854, when the city was founded along the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") by speculators from neighboring [Council Bluffs, Iowa](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"). It originally comprised a crossing called [Lone Tree Ferry](/wiki/Lone_Tree_Ferry "Lone Tree Ferry") earning the city its nickname, the "Gateway to the West". Omaha introduced this new West to the world in 1898, when it played host to the World's Fair, dubbed the [Trans-Mississippi Exposition](/wiki/Trans-Mississippi_Exposition "Trans-Mississippi Exposition"). During the 19th century, Omaha's central location in the United States spurred the city to become an important national [transportation hub](/wiki/Transportation_in_Omaha "Transportation in Omaha"). Throughout the rest of the 19th century, the transportation and [jobbing](/wiki/Wholesale "Wholesale") sectors were important in the city, along with its [railroads](/wiki/Railroads_in_Omaha "Railroads in Omaha") and [breweries](/wiki/Breweries "Breweries"). In the 20th century, the [Omaha Stockyards](/wiki/Omaha_Stockyards "Omaha Stockyards"), once the world's largest, and its [meatpacking](/wiki/Meatpacking "Meatpacking") plants gained international prominence.

Omaha is the home to the headquarters of four [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies: [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"), [Kiewit Corporation](/wiki/Kiewit_Corporation "Kiewit Corporation"), [Mutual of Omaha](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha "Mutual of Omaha"), and [Union Pacific Corporation](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Corporation "Union Pacific Corporation").[[10]](#cite_note-11) Other companies headquartered in the city include [First National Bank of Omaha](/wiki/First_National_Bank_of_Omaha "First National Bank of Omaha"), [Gallup, Inc.](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc."), [Green Plains](/wiki/Green_Plains "Green Plains"), [Intrado](/wiki/Intrado "Intrado"), [Valmont Industries](/wiki/Valmont_Industries "Valmont Industries"), [Werner Enterprises](/wiki/Werner_Enterprises "Werner Enterprises"), [WoodmenLife](/wiki/WoodmenLife "WoodmenLife"), and three of the nation's ten largest architecture and engineering firms ([DLR Group](/wiki/DLR_Group "DLR Group"), [HDR, Inc.](/wiki/HDR,_Inc. "HDR, Inc."), and [Leo A Daly](/wiki/Leo_A_Daly "Leo A Daly")).[[11]](#cite_note-BDC_2010-12) Notable cultural institutions include the [Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium](/wiki/Henry_Doorly_Zoo_and_Aquarium "Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium"), [Old Market](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)"), [Durham Museum](/wiki/Durham_Museum,_Omaha,_Nebraska "Durham Museum, Omaha, Nebraska"), [Lauritzen Gardens](/wiki/Lauritzen_Gardens "Lauritzen Gardens"), and annual [College World Series](/wiki/College_World_Series "College World Series"). Modern Omaha inventions include the [Reuben sandwich](/wiki/Reuben_sandwich "Reuben sandwich");[[12]](#cite_note-13) cake mix, developed by [Duncan Hines](/wiki/Duncan_Hines "Duncan Hines"); [center-pivot irrigation](/wiki/Center-pivot_irrigation "Center-pivot irrigation");[[13]](#cite_note-14) [Raisin Bran](/wiki/Raisin_Bran "Raisin Bran"); the first [ski lift](/wiki/Ski_lift "Ski lift") in the U.S.;[[14]](#cite_note-15) the [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40") radio format as first used in the U.S. at Omaha's [KOWH Radio](/wiki/KCRO#History "KCRO"); and the [TV dinner](/wiki/TV_dinner "TV dinner").[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/History_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "History of Omaha, Nebraska")

See also: [History of North Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/History_of_North_Omaha,_Nebraska "History of North Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Logan_fontenelle.jpg/250px-Logan_fontenelle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Logan_fontenelle.jpg)

[Logan Fontenelle](/wiki/Logan_Fontenelle "Logan Fontenelle"), an interpreter for the [Omaha Tribe](/wiki/Omaha_Tribe "Omaha Tribe") when it ceded the land that became the city of Omaha to the U.S. government

Various [Native American tribes](/wiki/Native_American_tribes_in_Nebraska "Native American tribes in Nebraska") had lived in the land that became Omaha since the 17th century, including the [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_people "Omaha people") and [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca"), [Dhegihan-Siouan language](/wiki/Dhegihan_languages "Dhegihan languages") people who had originated in the lower [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") valley and migrated west by the early 17th century; [Pawnee](/wiki/Pawnee_people "Pawnee people"), [Otoe](/wiki/Otoe "Otoe"), [Missouria](/wiki/Missouria "Missouria"), and [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_people "Iowa people"). The word *Omaha* ([Omaha–Ponca](/wiki/Omaha%E2%80%93Ponca_language "Omaha–Ponca language"): *Umoⁿhoⁿ* or *Umaⁿhaⁿ*) in the Omaha language means 'Upstream People' or 'Against the Current'.

In 1804 the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") passed the riverbanks where the city of Omaha would be built. Between July 30 and August 3, 1804, members of the expedition, including [Meriwether Lewis](/wiki/Meriwether_Lewis "Meriwether Lewis") and [William Clark](/wiki/William_Clark "William Clark"), met with Oto and Missouria tribal leaders at the [Council Bluff](/wiki/Council_Bluff,_Nebraska "Council Bluff, Nebraska") at a point about 20 mi (32 km) north of present-day Omaha.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Immediately south of that area, Americans built several fur trading outposts in succeeding years, including [Fort Lisa](/wiki/Fort_Lisa_(Nebraska) "Fort Lisa (Nebraska)") in 1812;[[17]](#cite_note-18) [Fort Atkinson](/wiki/Fort_Atkinson_(Nebraska) "Fort Atkinson (Nebraska)") in 1819;[[18]](#cite_note-19) [Cabanné's Trading Post](/wiki/Cabann%C3%A9%27s_Trading_Post "Cabanné's Trading Post"), built in 1822, and [Fontenelle's Post](/wiki/Fontenelle%27s_Post "Fontenelle's Post") in 1823, in what became [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Nebraska "Bellevue, Nebraska").[[19]](#cite_note-20) There was fierce competition among fur traders until [John Jacob Astor](/wiki/John_Jacob_Astor "John Jacob Astor") created the monopoly of the [American Fur Company](/wiki/American_Fur_Company "American Fur Company"). The [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons") built a town called [Cutler's Park](/wiki/Cutler%27s_Park "Cutler's Park") in the area in 1846.[[20]](#cite_note-21) While it was temporary, the settlement provided the basis for further development.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Through 26 separate treaties with the United States federal government, [Native American tribes in Nebraska](/wiki/Native_American_tribes_in_Nebraska "Native American tribes in Nebraska") gradually ceded the lands that now make up the state. The treaty and cession involving the Omaha area occurred in 1854 when the [Omaha Tribe](/wiki/Omaha_Tribe "Omaha Tribe") ceded most of east-central Nebraska.[[22]](#cite_note-23) [Logan Fontenelle](/wiki/Logan_Fontenelle "Logan Fontenelle"), an interpreter for the Omaha and signatory to the 1854 treaty, played an essential role in those proceedings.

### Pioneer Omaha

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pioneer Omaha")]

Before it was legal to claim land in [Indian Country](/wiki/Indian_Country "Indian Country"), [William D. Brown](/wiki/William_D._Brown "William D. Brown") operated the [Lone Tree Ferry](/wiki/Lone_Tree_Ferry "Lone Tree Ferry") that brought settlers from Council Bluffs, Iowa, to the area that became Omaha. Brown is generally credited as having the first vision for a city where Omaha now sits.[[23]](#cite_note-Administration1-24) The passage of the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act") in 1854 was presaged by the staking out of claims around the area to become Omaha by residents from neighboring Council Bluffs. On July 4, 1854, the city was informally established at a picnic on Capital Hill, current site of [Omaha Central High School](/wiki/Omaha_Central_High_School "Omaha Central High School").[[24]](#cite_note-25) Soon after, the [Omaha Claim Club](/wiki/Omaha_Claim_Club "Omaha Claim Club") was formed to provide [vigilante](/wiki/Vigilante "Vigilante") justice for [claim jumpers](/wiki/Land_claim "Land claim") and others who infringed on the land of many of [the city's founding fathers](/wiki/Founding_figures_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "Founding figures of Omaha, Nebraska").[[25]](#cite_note-26) Some of this land, which now wraps around Downtown Omaha, was later used to entice [Nebraska Territorial legislators](/wiki/Nebraska_Territorial_Legislature "Nebraska Territorial Legislature") to an area called [Scriptown](/wiki/Scriptown "Scriptown").[[26]](#cite_note-27) The Territorial capitol was in Omaha, but when Nebraska became a state in 1867, the capital was relocated to [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_Nebraska "Lincoln, Nebraska"), 53 mi (85 km) southwest of Omaha.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") later ruled against numerous landowners whose violent actions were condemned in *[Baker v. Morton](/wiki/Baker_v._Morton "Baker v. Morton")*.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Many of Omaha's founding figures stayed at the [Douglas House](/wiki/Douglas_House_(Omaha) "Douglas House (Omaha)") or the [Cozzens House Hotel](/wiki/Cozzens_House_Hotel "Cozzens House Hotel").[[29]](#cite_note-30) [Dodge Street](/wiki/Dodge_Street "Dodge Street") was important early in the city's early commercial history; [North 24th Street](/wiki/North_24th_Street "North 24th Street") and [South 24th Street](/wiki/South_Omaha_Main_Street_Historic_District "South Omaha Main Street Historic District") also developed independently as business districts. Early pioneers were buried in [Prospect Hill Cemetery](/wiki/Prospect_Hill_Cemetery_(North_Omaha,_Nebraska) "Prospect Hill Cemetery (North Omaha, Nebraska)") and Cedar Hill Cemetery.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Cedar Hill closed in the 1860s and its graves were moved to Prospect Hill, where pioneers were later joined by soldiers from [Fort Omaha](/wiki/Fort_Omaha "Fort Omaha"), [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "African Americans in Omaha, Nebraska") and early [European immigrants](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska").[[31]](#cite_note-32) There are several other [historical cemeteries in Omaha](/wiki/Cemeteries_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Cemeteries in Omaha, Nebraska"), historical [Jewish synagogues](/wiki/Jews_and_Judaism_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Jews and Judaism in Omaha, Nebraska") and historical [Christian churches](/wiki/Christianity_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Christianity in Omaha, Nebraska") dating from the pioneer era, as well. Two sculpture parks, Pioneer Courage and Spirit of Nebraska's Wilderness and [The Transcontinental Railroad](/wiki/Transcontinental_Railroad "Transcontinental Railroad"), celebrate the city's pioneering history.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Hotelfontenelle.jpg/250px-Hotelfontenelle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotelfontenelle.jpg)

[Hotel Fontenelle](/wiki/Hotel_Fontenelle "Hotel Fontenelle"), formerly in downtown Omaha

The [economy of Omaha](/wiki/Economy_of_Omaha "Economy of Omaha") boomed and busted through its early years. In 1858, the *Omaha Daily Republican* was founded by the *Omaha Printing Company (rebranded Aradius Group, 2016)*, it was Nebraska's first regional newspaper–founded before Nebraska claimed statehood. Omaha was a stopping point for settlers and prospectors heading west, either overland or by the Missouri River. The steamboat *[Bertrand](/wiki/Bertrand_(steamboat) "Bertrand (steamboat)")* sank north of Omaha on its way to the goldfields in 1865. Its massive collection of artifacts is on display at the nearby [Desoto National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Desoto_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Desoto National Wildlife Refuge"). The [jobbing and wholesaling district](/wiki/Jobber%27s_Canyon "Jobber's Canyon") brought new jobs, followed by the [railroads](/wiki/Railroads_in_Omaha "Railroads in Omaha") and the stockyards.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Groundbreaking for the [First transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") in 1863, provided an essential developmental boom for the city.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In 1862, the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") allowed the [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") to begin building westward railways;[[35]](#cite_note-Union_Pacific_RR_1867-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) in January 1866 it commenced construction out of Omaha.[[37]](#cite_note-Dunbar-38)

The [Union Stockyards](/wiki/Union_Stockyards_(Omaha) "Union Stockyards (Omaha)"), another important part of the city's development, were founded in South Omaha in 1883.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Within 20 years, Omaha had four of the five major [meatpacking](/wiki/Meatpacking "Meatpacking") companies in the United States. By the 1950s, half the city's workforce was employed in meatpacking and processing. Meatpacking, jobbing and railroads were responsible for most of the growth in the city from the late 19th century through the early decades of the 20th century.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

[Immigrants](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska") soon created [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") throughout the city, including Irish in [Sheelytown](/wiki/Sheelytown "Sheelytown") in South Omaha; Germans in the [Near North Side](/wiki/Near_North_Side_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Near North Side (Omaha, Nebraska)"), joined by the European Jews and black migrants from [the South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"); and [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Little Italy (Omaha, Nebraska)") and [Little Bohemia](/wiki/Little_Bohemia_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Little Bohemia (Omaha, Nebraska)") in South Omaha.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Beginning in the late 19th century, Omaha's upper class lived in posh enclaves throughout the city, including the south and [north](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Historic_District_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Gold Coast Historic District (Omaha, Nebraska)") Gold Coast neighborhoods, [Bemis Park](/wiki/Bemis_Park "Bemis Park"), [Kountze Place](/wiki/Kountze_Place "Kountze Place"), [Field Club](/wiki/Field_Club "Field Club") and throughout [Midtown Omaha](/wiki/Midtown_Omaha "Midtown Omaha"). They traveled the city's sprawling [park system](/wiki/Parks_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Parks in Omaha, Nebraska") on [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevards_in_Omaha "Boulevards in Omaha") designed by renowned [landscape architect](/wiki/Landscape_architect "Landscape architect") [Horace Cleveland](/wiki/Horace_Cleveland "Horace Cleveland").[[41]](#cite_note-42) The [Omaha Horse Railway](/wiki/Omaha_Horse_Railway "Omaha Horse Railway") first carried passengers throughout the city, as did the later [Omaha Cable Tramway Company](/wiki/Omaha_Cable_Tramway_Company "Omaha Cable Tramway Company") and several similar companies. In 1888, the [Omaha and Council Bluffs Railway and Bridge Company](/wiki/Omaha_and_Council_Bluffs_Railway_and_Bridge_Company "Omaha and Council Bluffs Railway and Bridge Company") built the [Douglas Street Bridge](/wiki/Douglas_Street_Bridge "Douglas Street Bridge"), the first pedestrian and wagon bridge between Omaha and Council Bluffs.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Gambling, drinking and prostitution were widespread in the 19th century, first rampant in the city's [Burnt District](/wiki/Burnt_District,_Omaha "Burnt District, Omaha") and later in the [Sporting District](/wiki/Sporting_District_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Sporting District (Omaha, Nebraska)").[[43]](#cite_note-44) Controlled by Omaha's [political boss](/wiki/Political_boss "Political boss") [Tom Dennison](/wiki/Tom_Dennison_(Political_boss) "Tom Dennison (Political boss)") by 1890, [criminal elements](/wiki/Crime_in_Omaha "Crime in Omaha") enjoyed support from Omaha's "perpetual" mayor, ["Cowboy Jim"](/wiki/James_Dahlman "James Dahlman") Dahlman, nicknamed for his eight terms as mayor.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Calamities such as the [Great Flood of 1881](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1881 "Great Flood of 1881") did not slow down the city's violence.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In 1882, the [Camp Dump Strike](/wiki/Camp_Dump_Strike "Camp Dump Strike") pitted state militia against unionized strikers, drawing national attention to Omaha's labor troubles. The [Governor of Nebraska](/wiki/Governor_of_Nebraska "Governor of Nebraska") had to call in [U.S. Army](/wiki/U.S._Army "U.S. Army") troops from nearby Fort Omaha to protect [strikebreakers](/wiki/Strikebreakers "Strikebreakers") for the [Burlington Railroad](/wiki/Burlington_Railroad "Burlington Railroad"), bringing along [Gatling guns](/wiki/Gatling_gun "Gatling gun") and a [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") for defense. When the event ended, one man was dead and several were wounded.[[47]](#cite_note-48) In 1891, a mob hanged [Joe Coe](/wiki/Joe_Coe "Joe Coe"), an African-American porter after he was accused of raping a white girl.[[48]](#cite_note-Bristow_2000-49) There were also several other [riots and civil unrest events in Omaha](/wiki/List_of_riots_and_civil_unrest_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of riots and civil unrest in Omaha, Nebraska") during this period.

In 1898, Omaha's leaders, under the guidance of [Gurdon Wattles](/wiki/Gurdon_Wattles "Gurdon Wattles"), held the [Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition](/wiki/Trans-Mississippi_and_International_Exposition "Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition"), touted as a celebration of agricultural and industrial growth throughout the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest").[[49]](#cite_note-50) The [Indian Congress](/wiki/Indian_Congress "Indian Congress"), which drew more than 500 [American Indians](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") from across the country, was held simultaneously. More than 2 million visitors attended these events at [Kountze Park](/wiki/Kountze_Park "Kountze Park") and the [Omaha Driving Park](/wiki/Omaha_Driving_Park "Omaha Driving Park") in the [Kountze Place](/wiki/Kountze_Place "Kountze Place") neighborhood.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

#### To the 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: To the 1960s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Illustrated_history_of_the_Union_Stockyards%3B_sketch-book_of_familiar_faces_and_places_at_the_yards_%281901%29_%2814765342124%29.jpg/250px-Illustrated_history_of_the_Union_Stockyards%3B_sketch-book_of_familiar_faces_and_places_at_the_yards_%281901%29_%2814765342124%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illustrated_history_of_the_Union_Stockyards;_sketch-book_of_familiar_faces_and_places_at_the_yards_(1901)_(14765342124).jpg)

Men working at Omaha's Union Stockyards

With dramatically increasing population in the 20th century, competition and fierce labor struggles led to major civil unrest.[[51]](#cite_note-52) In 1900, Omaha was the center of a national uproar over the [kidnapping](/wiki/Kidnap "Kidnap") of [Edward Cudahy, Jr.](/wiki/Edward_Cudahy,_Jr. "Edward Cudahy, Jr."), the son of a local [meatpacking](/wiki/Meatpacking "Meatpacking") magnate.[[52]](#cite_note-53) The city's labor and management clashed in bitter strikes, [racial tension](/wiki/Racial_tension_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Racial tension in Omaha, Nebraska") escalated as blacks were hired as strikebreakers, and ethnic strife broke out.[[53]](#cite_note-54) A [major riot by earlier immigrants](/wiki/Greek_Town_riot "Greek Town riot") in South Omaha destroyed the city's [Greek Town](/wiki/Greek_Town "Greek Town") in 1909, completely driving out the Greek population.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

The [civil rights movement in Omaha](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Civil Rights Movement in Omaha, Nebraska") traces to 1912, when the first chapter of the [National Association for the Advancement of Colored People](/wiki/National_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Colored_People "National Association for the Advancement of Colored People") west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") was founded in the city.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The [Omaha Easter Sunday Tornado of 1913](/wiki/Omaha_Easter_Sunday_Tornado_(1913) "Omaha Easter Sunday Tornado (1913)") destroyed much of the city's [African-American](/wiki/African_Americans_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "African Americans in Omaha, Nebraska") community, in addition to much of Midtown Omaha.[[56]](#cite_note-57) During that year, future [United States President](/wiki/United_States_President "United States President") [Gerald R. Ford](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford "Gerald R. Ford") was born in Omaha.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Today, there is a [museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Birthsite_and_Gardens "Gerald R. Ford Birthsite and Gardens") dedicated to his birthplace.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

In 1919, the city was caught up in the [Red Summer](/wiki/Red_Summer "Red Summer") riots when thousands of whites marched from South Omaha to the courthouse to lynch a black worker, Willy Brown, a suspect in an alleged rape of a white woman. The mob burned the [Douglas County Courthouse](/wiki/Douglas_County_Courthouse_(Nebraska) "Douglas County Courthouse (Nebraska)") to get the prisoner, causing more than $1 million damage. They hanged and shot Will Brown, then burned his body.[[59]](#cite_note-60) Troops were called in from Fort Omaha to quell the riot, prevent more crowds gathering in South Omaha, and to protect the black community in North Omaha.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

The [culture of North Omaha](/wiki/Culture_in_North_Omaha,_Nebraska "Culture in North Omaha, Nebraska") thrived throughout the 1920s through 1950s, with several creative figures, including [Tillie Olsen](/wiki/Tillie_Olsen "Tillie Olsen"), [Wallace Thurman](/wiki/Wallace_Thurman "Wallace Thurman"), [Lloyd Hunter](/wiki/Lloyd_Hunter "Lloyd Hunter"), and [Anna Mae Winburn](/wiki/Anna_Mae_Winburn "Anna Mae Winburn") emerging from the vibrant Near North Side.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Musicians created their own world in Omaha, and also joined national bands and groups that toured and appeared in the city.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

After the tumultuous [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s, Omaha rebounded with the development of [Offutt Air Force Base](/wiki/Offutt_Air_Force_Base "Offutt Air Force Base") just south of the city. The [Glenn L. Martin Company](/wiki/Glenn_L._Martin_Company "Glenn L. Martin Company") operated a factory there in the 1940s that produced 521 [B-29](/wiki/B-29 "B-29") *Superfortresses*, including the *[Enola Gay](/wiki/Enola_Gay "Enola Gay")* and *[Bockscar](/wiki/Bockscar "Bockscar")* used in [the atomic bombing of Japan](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") in World War II.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The construction of [Interstates](/wiki/Interstates "Interstates") [80](/wiki/Interstate_80 "Interstate 80"), [480](/wiki/Interstate_480_(Iowa%E2%80%93Nebraska) "Interstate 480 (Iowa–Nebraska)") and [680](/wiki/Interstate_680_(Iowa%E2%80%93Nebraska) "Interstate 680 (Iowa–Nebraska)"), along with the [North Omaha Freeway](/wiki/North_Omaha_Freeway "North Omaha Freeway"), spurred development. There was also controversy, particularly in North Omaha, where new routes bisected several neighborhoods.[[64]](#cite_note-65) [Creighton University](/wiki/Creighton_University "Creighton University") hosted the [DePorres Club](/wiki/DePorres_Club "DePorres Club"), an early civil rights group whose use of sit-in strategies for integration of public facilities predated the national movement.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Following the development of the Glenn L. Martin Company bomber manufacturing plant in [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Nebraska "Bellevue, Nebraska") at the beginning of World War II, the relocation of the [Strategic Air Command](/wiki/Strategic_Air_Command "Strategic Air Command") to the Omaha suburb in 1948 provided a major economic boost to the area.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

Insurance boomed from the 1950s through the 1960s: more than 40 insurance companies were headquartered in Omaha, including [Woodmen of the World](/wiki/Woodmen_of_the_World "Woodmen of the World") and [Mutual of Omaha](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha "Mutual of Omaha"). The city rivaled but never surpassed the United States insurance centers of [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), New York City, and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston").[[67]](#cite_note-68) After surpassing Chicago in [meat processing](/wiki/Meat_processing "Meat processing") by the late 1950s, Omaha suffered the loss of 10,000 jobs as both the railroad and meatpacking industries restructured. The city struggled for decades to shift its economy as workers suffered. Poverty became more entrenched among families who remained in North Omaha. In the 1960s, three major race riots along [North 24th Street](/wiki/North_24th_Street "North 24th Street") destroyed the Near North Side's economic base, with recovery slow for decades.[[68]](#cite_note-69) In 1969, [Woodmen Tower](/wiki/Woodmen_Tower "Woodmen Tower") was completed and became Omaha's tallest building and first major skyscraper at 478 ft (146 m), a sign of renewal.

#### 1970s and onwards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1970s and onwards")]

Since the 1970s, Omaha has continued expanding and growing, mostly to available land to the west. [West Omaha](/wiki/West_Omaha "West Omaha") has become home to the majority of the city's population. North and South Omaha's populations continue to be centers of new immigrants, with economic and racial diversity. In 1975 a [major tornado](/wiki/1975_Omaha_tornado_outbreak "1975 Omaha tornado outbreak"), along with a [major blizzard](/wiki/Great_Storm_of_1975 "Great Storm of 1975"), caused more than [$](/wiki/$ "$")100 million in damages in 1975 dollars.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

Downtown Omaha has since been rejuvenated in numerous ways, starting with the development of [Gene Leahy Mall](/wiki/Gene_Leahy_Mall "Gene Leahy Mall")[[70]](#cite_note-71) and [W. Dale Clark Library](/wiki/W._Dale_Clark_Library "W. Dale Clark Library")[[71]](#cite_note-72) in the late 1970s. In the 1980s, Omaha's fruit warehouses were converted into a shopping area called the [Old Market](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)"). The demolition of [Jobber's Canyon](/wiki/Jobber%27s_Canyon "Jobber's Canyon") in 1989 led to the creation of the [ConAgra Foods](/wiki/ConAgra_Foods "ConAgra Foods") campus.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Several nearby buildings, including the [Nash Block](/wiki/Nash_Block "Nash Block"), have been converted into condominiums. The stockyards were taken down; the only surviving building is the [Livestock Exchange Building](/wiki/Livestock_Exchange_Building_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Livestock Exchange Building (Omaha, Nebraska)"), which was converted to multi-use and listed on the National Register of Historic Places.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

A [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") movement in Omaha has led to a number of historic structures and districts being designated [Omaha Landmarks](/wiki/Omaha_Landmark "Omaha Landmark") or listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). Much of the push toward preservation came after Omaha gained the notorious designation of having, in 1989, demolished the largest-ever [National Register](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") historic district in the United States, a record that still stands as of 2013. The [Jobbers Canyon Historic District](/wiki/Jobbers_Canyon_Historic_District "Jobbers Canyon Historic District"), along the Missouri River, was felled for a new headquarters campus for ConAgra Foods, a company which threatened to relocate if Omaha did not allow them to raze the city's historic district. The Jobber's Canyon warehouses had before then been allowed to deteriorate and were the scene of several fires set by the homeless population that had come to live in the abandoned buildings. At the time, there were no plans in place for revitalizing the buildings.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

In the 1980s and 1990s, Omaha also saw major company headquarters leave the city, including [Enron](/wiki/Enron "Enron"), founded in the city in 1930 and taken to Houston in 1987 by the now-notorious [Kenneth Lay](/wiki/Kenneth_Lay "Kenneth Lay"). [First Data](/wiki/First_Data "First Data") Corporation, a large credit-card processor, also was founded in Omaha in 1969; as of 2009, its headquarters are in Atlanta.

[Inacom](/wiki/Inacom "Inacom"), founded in Omaha in 1991, was a technology company that customized computer systems for large businesses, and was on the Fortune 500 list from 1997 until 2000, when it filed for bankruptcy. [Northwestern Bell](/wiki/Northwestern_Bell "Northwestern Bell"), the [Bell System](/wiki/Bell_System "Bell System") affiliate for Northwestern states, had its headquarters in Omaha from its founding in 1896 until it moved to Denver in 1991 as [US West](/wiki/US_West "US West"). [Level 3 Communications](/wiki/Level_3_Communications "Level 3 Communications"), a large [Tier 1 network](/wiki/Tier_1_network "Tier 1 network") provider, was founded in Omaha in 1985 as Kiewit Diversified Group, a division of [Kiewit Corporation](/wiki/Kiewit_Corporation "Kiewit Corporation"), a Fortune 500 construction and mining company still headquartered in Omaha; Level 3 moved to Denver in 1998. [World Com](/wiki/World_Com "World Com") was founded by a merger with Omaha's MFS Communications, started as [Metropolitan Fiber Systems](/wiki/Metropolitan_Fiber_Systems "Metropolitan Fiber Systems") in 1993. MFS, backed by [Kiewit Corporation](/wiki/Kiewit_Corporation "Kiewit Corporation") CEO [Walter Scott Jr.](/wiki/Walter_Scott_Jr. "Walter Scott Jr.") and [Warren Buffett](/wiki/Warren_Buffett "Warren Buffett"), purchased [UUNET](/wiki/UUNET "UUNET"), one of the largest Internet backbones in the world, for $2 billion in 1996. The now-infamous [Bernie Ebbers](/wiki/Bernie_Ebbers "Bernie Ebbers") purchased the much larger MFS for $14.3 billion in 1997 under his [World Com](/wiki/World_Com "World Com"). He moved headquarters of the merged company from Omaha to Mississippi.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/First_National_Tower.jpg/250px-First_National_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_National_Tower.jpg)

[First National Bank Tower](/wiki/First_National_Bank_Tower "First National Bank Tower"), the tallest building in Omaha

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Omaha-Night-10th-Capitol-Spencer-Hession.jpg/250px-Omaha-Night-10th-Capitol-Spencer-Hession.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omaha-Night-10th-Capitol-Spencer-Hession.jpg)

Omaha at night, in 2021, as seen from the intersection of 10th and Capitol streets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Bob_Kerrey_Pedestrian_Bridge_%282010%29.jpg/250px-Bob_Kerrey_Pedestrian_Bridge_%282010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Kerrey_Pedestrian_Bridge_(2010).jpg)

[Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge](/wiki/Bob_Kerrey_Pedestrian_Bridge "Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge")

Around the start of the 21st century, several downtown skyscrapers and cultural institutions were built.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The [First National Bank Tower](/wiki/First_National_Bank_Tower "First National Bank Tower") on Dodge Street was completed in 2002 and is the [tallest building in Omaha](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of tallest buildings in Omaha, Nebraska") and [the state](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Nebraska "List of tallest buildings in Nebraska"), surpassing the [Woodmen Tower](/wiki/Woodmen_Tower "Woodmen Tower") as the tallest in both at 634 ft (193 m). The creation of the city's new [North Downtown](/wiki/NoDo "NoDo") included the construction of the [CenturyLink Center](/wiki/CenturyLink_Center_Omaha "CenturyLink Center Omaha") and the [Slowdown](/wiki/Slowdown_(venue) "Slowdown (venue)")/[Film Streams](/wiki/Film_Streams "Film Streams") development at North 14th and Webster Streets.[[79]](#cite_note-80) Construction of the new [TD Ameritrade Park](/wiki/TD_Ameritrade_Park "TD Ameritrade Park"), also in the North Downtown area, began in 2009 and was completed in 2011. TD Ameritrade Park is now the home of the [College World Series](/wiki/College_World_Series "College World Series"), an event tourists flock to each year. The [Union Pacific Center](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Center "Union Pacific Center") and the [Holland Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Holland_Performing_Arts_Center "Holland Performing Arts Center") opened in 2004 and 2005, respectively.

Important retail and office developments occurred in West Omaha, such as the Village Pointe shopping center and several business parks.[[80]](#cite_note-81) The site of the former [Ak-Sar-Ben](/wiki/Ak-Sar-Ben_(arena) "Ak-Sar-Ben (arena)") arena was redeveloped into a [mixed-use development](/wiki/Mixed-use_development "Mixed-use development") [Aksarben Village](/wiki/Aksarben_Village "Aksarben Village"). In January 2009, [Blue Cross Blue Shield of Nebraska](/wiki/Blue_Cross_and_Blue_Shield_Association "Blue Cross and Blue Shield Association") announced plans to build a 10 story, $98 million headquarters in the [Aksarben Village](/wiki/Aksarben_Village "Aksarben Village") which it completed in Spring 2011.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Another major mixed-use development to come to Omaha was [Midtown Crossing at Turner Park](/wiki/Midtown_Crossing_at_Turner_Park "Midtown Crossing at Turner Park"). Developed by [Mutual of Omaha](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha "Mutual of Omaha"), the development includes several condominium towers and retail businesses built around Omaha's Turner Park.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-bydesign-84)

There have also been several developments along the Missouri River waterfront near downtown. The [Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge](/wiki/Bob_Kerrey_Pedestrian_Bridge "Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge") was opened to foot and bicycle traffic on September 28, 2008.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Started in 2003,[[85]](#cite_note-86) [RiverFront Place Condos](/wiki/RiverFront_Place_Condos "RiverFront Place Condos") first phase was completed in 2006 and the second phase was opened in 2011. The development along Omaha's riverfront is attributed with prompting the City of Council Bluffs to move their own riverfront development time line forward.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

In the summers of 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2021 the [United States Olympic Team](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Summer_Olympics "United States at the Summer Olympics") swimming trials were held in Omaha, at the Qwest/Century Link Center.[[87]](#cite_note-swimmingteam-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89) These events were highlights in [the city's sports community](/wiki/Sports_in_Omaha "Sports in Omaha"),[[89]](#cite_note-90) as well as a showcase for redevelopment in the downtown area.

In the 2020s, a number of large projects have been either completed or planned in an attempt to revitalize downtown Omaha.[[90]](#cite_note-91) These include the redevelopment of the [Gene Leahy Mall](/wiki/Gene_Leahy_Mall "Gene Leahy Mall"), a large park near Omaha's Riverfront,[[91]](#cite_note-92) and the [Omaha Streetcar](/wiki/Omaha_Streetcar "Omaha Streetcar"), a nearly $500 million system of public transit.[[92]](#cite_note-93) A new skyscraper, the [Mutual of Omaha Headquarters Tower](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha_Headquarters_Tower "Mutual of Omaha Headquarters Tower"), at 677 feet (206 m), will be the new [tallest building in Omaha](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of tallest buildings in Omaha, Nebraska") and [the state](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Nebraska "List of tallest buildings in Nebraska") upon its completion in 2026.

Douglas County treasurer [John Ewing](/wiki/John_Ewing_Jr. "John Ewing Jr.") was [elected mayor](/wiki/2025_Omaha_mayoral_election "2025 Omaha mayoral election") in 2025, ending Stothert's 12-year long administration. He is the first black mayor of Omaha.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [Geography of Omaha](/wiki/Geography_of_Omaha "Geography of Omaha") and [Omaha-Council Bluffs metropolitan area](/wiki/Omaha-Council_Bluffs_metropolitan_area "Omaha-Council Bluffs metropolitan area")

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 130.58 sq mi (338.20 km2), of which 127.09 sq mi (329.16 km2) is land and 3.49 sq mi (9.04 km2) is water.[[94]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-95) Situated in the Midwestern United States on the bank of the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") in eastern Nebraska, much of Omaha is built in the [Missouri River Valley](/wiki/Missouri_River_Valley "Missouri River Valley"). Other significant bodies of water in the Omaha-Council Bluffs metropolitan area include Lake Manawa, [Papillion Creek](/wiki/Papillion_Creek "Papillion Creek"), [Carter Lake](/wiki/Carter_Lake_(Iowa%E2%80%93Nebraska) "Carter Lake (Iowa–Nebraska)"), Platte River and the [Glenn Cunningham Lake](/wiki/Glenn_Cunningham_Lake "Glenn Cunningham Lake"). The city's land has been altered considerably with substantial [land grading](/wiki/Land_grading "Land grading") throughout Downtown Omaha and scattered across the city.[[95]](#cite_note-96) [East Omaha](/wiki/East_Omaha "East Omaha") sits on a [flood plain](/wiki/Flood_plain "Flood plain") west of the Missouri River. The area is the location of Carter Lake, an [oxbow lake](/wiki/Oxbow_lake "Oxbow lake"). The lake was once the site of East Omaha Island and Florence Lake, which dried up in the 1920s.

### Metropolitan area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Metropolitan area")]

The Omaha-Council Bluffs metropolitan area consists of eight counties; five in Nebraska and three in Iowa.[[96]](#cite_note-97) The metropolitan area now includes [Harrison](/wiki/Harrison_County,_Iowa "Harrison County, Iowa"), [Pottawattamie](/wiki/Pottawattamie_County,_Iowa "Pottawattamie County, Iowa"), and [Mills](/wiki/Mills_County,_Iowa "Mills County, Iowa") Counties in Iowa and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_County,_Nebraska "Washington County, Nebraska"), Douglas, [Sarpy](/wiki/Sarpy_County,_Nebraska "Sarpy County, Nebraska"), [Cass](/wiki/Cass_County,_Nebraska "Cass County, Nebraska"), and [Saunders](/wiki/Saunders_County,_Nebraska "Saunders County, Nebraska") Counties in Nebraska. This area was formerly referred to only as the Omaha Metropolitan Statistical Area and consisted of only five counties: Pottawattamie in Iowa, and Washington, Douglas, Cass, and Sarpy in Nebraska.[[97]](#cite_note-98) The Omaha-Council Bluffs [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") comprises the Omaha-Council Bluffs [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") and the [Fremont](/wiki/Fremont,_Nebraska "Fremont, Nebraska") [Micropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area"); the CSA has a population of 858,720 (2005 Census Bureau estimate). Omaha ranks as the [41st-most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), and is the core city of its 60th-largest metropolitan area.[[98]](#cite_note-99) There are no [consolidated city-counties](/wiki/Consolidated_city-counties "Consolidated city-counties") in the area; the [City of Omaha](/wiki/Government_of_Omaha "Government of Omaha") studied the possibility extensively through 2003 and concluded, "The City of Omaha and Douglas County should merge into a municipal county, work to commence immediately, and that functional consolidations begin immediately in as many departments as possible, including but not limited to parks, [fleet](/wiki/Fleet_vehicle "Fleet vehicle") management, facilities management, [local planning](/wiki/Local_planning "Local planning"), purchasing and personnel."[[99]](#cite_note-100)

Geographically, Omaha is considered as being in the "Heartland" of the United States. Important environmental impacts on the natural habitat in the area include the spread of [invasive plant](/wiki/Invasive_plant "Invasive plant") species, restoring [prairies](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") and [bur oak](/wiki/Bur_oak "Bur oak") [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") habitats, and managing the [whitetail deer](/wiki/Whitetail_deer "Whitetail deer") population.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

Omaha is home to several hospitals, mostly along Dodge Street (US6). Being the county seat, it is also the location of the county courthouse.

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Main article: [Neighborhoods of Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "Neighborhoods of Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Omaha_neighborhoods.png/250px-Omaha_neighborhoods.png)](/wiki/File:Omaha_neighborhoods.png)

Downtown: lime, Midtown: blue-gray, North: red, South: pink, West: lavender

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Above_West_Omaha.jpg/250px-Above_West_Omaha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Above_West_Omaha.jpg)

View from above West Omaha

Omaha is generally divided into six geographic areas: Downtown, Midtown, North Omaha, South Omaha, West Omaha, and East Omaha. West Omaha includes the Miracle Hills, [Boys Town](/wiki/Boys_Town,_Nebraska "Boys Town, Nebraska"), Regency, and Gateway areas.[[83]](#cite_note-bydesign-84) The city has a wide range of historical and new neighborhoods and suburbs that reflect its [socioeconomic](/wiki/Socioeconomic "Socioeconomic") diversity. Early neighborhood development happened in ethnic enclaves,[[101]](#cite_note-102) including [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Little Italy (Omaha, Nebraska)"), [Little Bohemia](/wiki/Little_Bohemia_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Little Bohemia (Omaha, Nebraska)"), Little Mexico and [Greek Town](/wiki/Greek_Town "Greek Town").[[102]](#cite_note-103) According to U.S. Census data, five European ethnic enclaves existed in Omaha in 1880, expanding to nine in 1900.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

Around the start of the 20th century. the City of Omaha annexed several surrounding communities, including [Florence](/wiki/Florence,_Nebraska "Florence, Nebraska"), [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee,_Nebraska "Dundee, Nebraska") and [Benson](/wiki/Benson,_Nebraska "Benson, Nebraska"). At the same time, the city annexed all of South Omaha, including the [Dahlman](/wiki/Dahlman_neighborhood "Dahlman neighborhood") and [Burlington Road neighborhoods](/wiki/Burlington_Road_neighborhood "Burlington Road neighborhood"). From its first annexation in 1857 (of East Omaha) to its controversial annexation of [Elkhorn](/wiki/Elkhorn,_Nebraska "Elkhorn, Nebraska") in 2007, Omaha has continually had an eye towards growth.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Starting in the 1950s, development of highways and new housing led to the movement of the middle class to [suburbs](/wiki/Suburbs "Suburbs") in West Omaha. Some of the movement was designated as [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") from racial unrest in the 1960s.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Newer and poorer migrants lived in older housing close to downtown; those residents who were more established moved west into newer housing. Some suburbs are [gated communities](/wiki/Gated_communities "Gated communities") or have become [edge cities](/wiki/Edge_cities "Edge cities").[[106]](#cite_note-107) Recently, Omahans have made strides to revitalize the downtown and Midtown areas with the redevelopment of the Old Market, Turner Park, Gifford Park, and the designation of the [Omaha Rail and Commerce Historic District](/wiki/Omaha_Rail_and_Commerce_Historic_District "Omaha Rail and Commerce Historic District").[[107]](#cite_note-108)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Monthly_Climate_Normals_%281991-2020%29_-_Omaha_Area%2C_NE%28ThreadEx%29.svg/250px-Monthly_Climate_Normals_%281991-2020%29_-_Omaha_Area%2C_NE%28ThreadEx%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Monthly_Climate_Normals_(1991-2020)_-_Omaha_Area,_NE(ThreadEx).svg)

Climate chart for Omaha

Omaha, due to its latitude of 41.26˚ N and location far from moderating bodies of water or mountain ranges, displays a hot-summer [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Dfa*).[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110) July averages 76.7 °F (24.8 °C), with average relative humidity around 70% which then leads to relatively frequent thunderstorms. Temperatures reach 90 °F (32 °C) on 29 days and 100 °F (38 °C) on 1.7 days annually. The January daily average is 23.5 °F (−4.7 °C), with lows reaching 0 °F (−18 °C) on 11 days annually. The lowest temperature recorded in the city was −32 °F (−35.6 °C) on January 5, 1884,[[110]](#cite_note-Jan_Averages-111) and the highest 114 °F (45.6 °C) on [July 25, 1936](/wiki/1936_North_American_heat_wave "1936 North American heat wave").[[111]](#cite_note-Jul_Averages-112) Average yearly precipitation is 30.6 in (777 mm), falling mostly in the warmer months. Snow is the most common precipitation in winter, with average seasonal snowfall being 28.7 in (72.9 cm).

Based on 30-year averages obtained from [NOAA](/wiki/NOAA "NOAA")'s [National Climatic Data Center](/wiki/National_Climatic_Data_Center "National Climatic Data Center") for the months of December, January and February, [Weather Channel](/wiki/Weather_Channel "Weather Channel") ranked Omaha the 5th coldest major U.S. city as of 2014.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")[[125]](#cite_note-129) counted 486,051 people, 189,922 households, and 113,245 families in Omaha. The population density was 3,406.8 per square mile (1,315.4/km2). There were 203,215 housing units, at an average density of 1,424.4 per square mile (550.0/km2). The racial makeup (including Hispanics in the racial counts) was 65.47% (318,218) [white](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 12.4% (60,280) [black or African-American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._census) "African American (U.S. census)"), 1.12% (5,426) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 4.6% (22,377) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.09% (461) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 7.25% (35,233) from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 9.06% (44,056) from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race was 14.0% (67,715) of the population.[[120]](#cite_note-census1-123)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Omaha_%285560454122%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Omaha_%285560454122%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Omaha_(5560454122).png)

Map of racial distribution in Omaha, 2010 U.S. Census. Each dot is 25 people: ⬤ White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Other

Of the 189,922 households, 28.3% had children under the age of 18; 43.0% were married couples living together; 29.2% had a female householder with no husband present. 33.2% of households consisted of individuals and 10.7% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.5 and the average family size was 3.2.

24.5% of the population was under the age of 18, 9.6% from 18 to 24, 28.2% from 25 to 44, 23.2% from 45 to 64, and 13.1% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 34.9 years. For every 100 females, the population had 97.1 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older, there were 95.0 males.

The 2016–2020 5-year [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey")[[126]](#cite_note-130) estimates show that the median household income was $62,213 (with a margin of error of +/- $969) and the median family income $80,956 (+/- $1,380). Males had a median income of $41,528 (+/- $592) versus $31,295 (+/- $490) for females. The median income for those above 16 years old was $36,290 (+/- $532). Approximately, 8.0% of families and 12.0% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 15.6% of those under the age of 18 and 7.8% of those ages 65 or over.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[127]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-131) of 2010, there were 408,958 people, 162,627 households, and 96,477 families residing in the city. The population density was 3,217.9/sq mi (1,242.4/km2). There were 177,518 housing units, at an average density of 1,396.8/sq mi (539.3/km2). The city's racial makeup was 73.1% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 13.7% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._census) "African American (U.S. census)"), 0.8% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 2.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 6.9% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 3.0% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") people of any race were 13.1% of the population. [Non-Hispanic Whites](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_Whites "Non-Hispanic Whites") were 68.0% of the population.[[120]](#cite_note-census1-123)

There were 162,627 households, of which 31.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 40.6% were married couples living together, 13.7% had a female householder with no husband present, 4.9% had a male householder with no wife present, and 40.7% were non-families. 32.3% of all households were made up of individuals, and 9.3% had someone living alone who was at least 65 years old. The average household size was 2.45 and the average family size was 3.14.

The median age in the city was 33.5 years. 25.1% of residents were under the age of 18; 11.4% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 27.9% were from 25 to 44; 24.4% were from 45 to 64; and 11.4% were 65 years of age or older. The city's gender makeup was 49.2% male and 50.8% female.

The median household income (in 2017 dollars) from 2013 to 2017 was $53,789.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [Crime in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Crime_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Crime in Omaha, Nebraska")

Further information: [Gambling in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Gambling_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Gambling in Omaha, Nebraska")

As a major industrial city into the mid-20th century, Omaha shared in social tensions that came with rapid growth and the arrival of large numbers of immigrants and migrants. Persistent poverty resulting from racial discrimination and job losses generated different crimes in the late 20th century, with the drug trade and drug abuse becoming associated with violent crime rates, which climbed after 1986 as Los Angeles gangs made affiliates in the city.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

Gambling in Omaha has been an important part of the city's history. From its founding in the 1850s through the 1930s, the city was known as a "wide-open" town where gambling of all sorts was openly accepted. By the 1950s, at the same time large-scale restructuring of the railroads, the meatpacking industry and other sectors caused widespread job losses and unemployment, Omaha reportedly had more illicit gambling than any other city in the nation.[[130]](#cite_note-134) From the 1930s through the 1970s, a Mafia-based criminal element controlled gambling in the city.[[131]](#cite_note-135)

As most forms of gambling are currently restricted in Nebraska, gambling in Omaha is limited to [keno](/wiki/Keno "Keno"), [lotteries](/wiki/Lotteries "Lotteries"), and [parimutuel betting](/wiki/Parimutuel_betting "Parimutuel betting"). This leaves Omahans to drive across the Missouri River to Council Bluffs, Iowa, where casinos are legal and many businesses operate. Recently, the [National Indian Gaming Commission](/wiki/National_Indian_Gaming_Commission "National Indian Gaming Commission") approved a controversial proposal made by the [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca") tribe of Nebraska. It will allow the tribe to build a casino in [Carter Lake, Iowa](/wiki/Carter_Lake,_Iowa "Carter Lake, Iowa"), which sits on the west side of the Missouri River, adjacent to Omaha, where casinos are illegal.[[132]](#cite_note-136)[[133]](#cite_note-137)[[134]](#cite_note-138)

### People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: People")]

Main article: [Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/OmahaNE_StCecilia.jpg/250px-OmahaNE_StCecilia.jpg)](/wiki/File:OmahaNE_StCecilia.jpg)

[Saint Cecilia Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Cecilia_Cathedral "Saint Cecilia Cathedral")

[Native Americans](/wiki/Native_American_tribes_in_Nebraska "Native American tribes in Nebraska") were the first residents of the Omaha area. The city of Omaha was established by white settlers from neighboring Council Bluffs who arrived from the [Mid-Atlantic states](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states "Mid-Atlantic states") a few years earlier. While much of the early population was of [Upland Southern](/wiki/Upland_South "Upland South") stock, over the next 100 years numerous [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska") moved to the city. In 1910, the Census Bureau reported Omaha's population as 96.4% White and 3.6% Black.[[135]](#cite_note-139) [Irish](/wiki/Irish_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Irish in Omaha, Nebraska") immigrants in Omaha originally moved to an area in present-day North Omaha called [Gophertown](/wiki/Gophertown "Gophertown"), as they lived in dug-out [sod houses](/wiki/Sod_house "Sod house").[[48]](#cite_note-Bristow_2000-49) That population was followed by [Polish immigrants](/wiki/Poles_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Poles in Omaha, Nebraska") in the [Sheelytown](/wiki/Sheelytown "Sheelytown") neighborhood, and many immigrants were recruited for jobs in South Omaha's [stockyards](/wiki/Union_Stockyards_(Omaha) "Union Stockyards (Omaha)") and meatpacking industry.[[136]](#cite_note-140) The [German community](/wiki/Germans_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Germans in Omaha, Nebraska") in Omaha was largely responsible for founding its once-thriving beer industry,[[137]](#cite_note-141) including the [Metz](/wiki/Metz_Brewery "Metz Brewery"), [Krug](/wiki/Krug_Brewery "Krug Brewery"), [Falstaff](/wiki/Falstaff_Brewing_Corporation "Falstaff Brewing Corporation") and [Storz](/wiki/Storz_Brewery "Storz Brewery") breweries.

Since its founding, [ethnic groups in the city](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Ethnic groups in Omaha, Nebraska") have clustered in [enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") in [north](/wiki/North_Omaha,_Nebraska "North Omaha, Nebraska"), [south](/wiki/South_Omaha,_Nebraska "South Omaha, Nebraska") and [downtown Omaha](/wiki/Downtown_Omaha "Downtown Omaha"). In its early days, the [sometimes lawless nature of a new frontier city](/wiki/History_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "History of Omaha, Nebraska") included [crime](/wiki/Crime_in_Omaha "Crime in Omaha"), such as [illicit gambling](/wiki/Gambling_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Gambling in Omaha, Nebraska") and [riots](/wiki/List_of_riots_and_civil_unrest_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of riots and civil unrest in Omaha, Nebraska").

In the early 20th century, [Jewish](/wiki/Jews_and_Judaism_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Jews and Judaism in Omaha, Nebraska") immigrants set up many businesses along the [North 24th Street](/wiki/North_24th_Street "North 24th Street") commercial area. It suffered with the loss of industrial jobs in the 1960s and, later, the shifting of population west of the city. The commercial area is now the center of the [African-American community](/wiki/African_Americans_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "African Americans in Omaha, Nebraska"), concentrated in North Omaha.[[138]](#cite_note-142) The African American community has maintained its social and religious base, while it is experiencing an economic revitalization.

The [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy_(Omaha) "Little Italy (Omaha)") neighborhood grew south of downtown, as many Italian immigrants came to the city to work in the [Union Pacific shops](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad_Omaha_Shops_Facility "Union Pacific Railroad Omaha Shops Facility").[[139]](#cite_note-143) Scandinavians first came to Omaha as [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon") settlers in the [Florence neighborhood](/wiki/Florence,_Nebraska "Florence, Nebraska").[[140]](#cite_note-144)[[141]](#cite_note-145) [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Czechs in Omaha, Nebraska") had a strong political and cultural voice in Omaha,[[142]](#cite_note-146) and were involved in a variety of trades and businesses, including banks, wholesale houses, and funeral homes. The [Notre Dame Academy and Convent](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Academy_and_Convent "Notre Dame Academy and Convent") and [Czechoslovak Museum](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Museum "Czechoslovak Museum") are legacies of their residence.[[143]](#cite_note-147) Today the legacy of the city's early European immigrant populations is evident in many social and cultural institutions in Downtown and South Omaha.

[Mexicans](/wiki/Mexicans_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Mexicans in Omaha, Nebraska") originally immigrated to Omaha to work in the rail yards. Today they account for most of South Omaha's Hispanic population and many have taken jobs in [meat processing](/wiki/Meat_processing "Meat processing").[[144]](#cite_note-148) Other large early ethnic populations in Omaha included [Danes](/wiki/Danes_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Danes in Omaha, Nebraska"), [Poles](/wiki/Poles_in_Omaha "Poles in Omaha"), and [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Swedes in Omaha, Nebraska").

A growing number of African immigrants have made their homes in Omaha in the last twenty years.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] There are approximately 8,500 [Sudanese](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") living in Omaha, including the largest population of [Sudanese refugees](/wiki/Sudanese_refugees "Sudanese refugees") in the United States. Most have immigrated since 1995 because of [warfare in Sudan](/wiki/Second_Sudanese_Civil_War "Second Sudanese Civil War"). They represent ten ethnic groups, including the [Nuer](/wiki/Nuer_people "Nuer people"), [Dinka](/wiki/Dinka "Dinka"), [Equatorians](/wiki/Equatoria "Equatoria"), [Maubans](/wiki/Mauban "Mauban") and [Nubians](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians"). Most Sudanese people in Omaha speak the [Nuer language](/wiki/Nuer_language "Nuer language").[[145]](#cite_note-149) Other Africans have immigrated to Omaha as well, with one-third from [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), and large populations from [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo"), [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") and [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana").[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151)[[148]](#cite_note-152)

With the expansion of railroad and industrial jobs in meatpacking, Omaha attracted many immigrants and migrants. As the major city in Nebraska, it has historically been more racially and ethnically diverse than the rest of the state.[[149]](#cite_note-153) At times rapid population change, overcrowded housing and job competition have aroused [racial and ethnic tensions](/wiki/Racial_Tension_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Racial Tension in Omaha, Nebraska"). Around the start of the 20th century, violence towards new immigrants in Omaha often erupted out of suspicion and fear.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

In 1909, anti-Greek sentiment flared after increased Greek immigration, and worsened their tendency to become [strikebreakers](/wiki/Strikebreakers "Strikebreakers"). The killing of a policeman of Irish descent enraged the Irish community; an angry mob violently stormed the Greek neighborhood in Omaha in what would become known as the [Greek Town Riot](/wiki/Greek_Town_Riot "Greek Town Riot").[[151]](#cite_note-155) That mob violence forced the [Greek](/wiki/Greeks_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Greeks in Omaha, Nebraska") immigrant population to flee from the city.[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157) By 1910, 53.7% of Omaha's residents and 64.2% of South Omaha's residents were foreign born or had at least one parent born outside of America.[[154]](#cite_note-158)

Six years after the Greek Town Riot, in 1915, a mob killed Juan Gonzalez, a Mexican immigrant, near [Scribner](/wiki/Scribner,_Nebraska "Scribner, Nebraska"), a town in the Greater Omaha metropolitan area. The event occurred after an [Omaha Police Department](/wiki/Omaha_Police_Department "Omaha Police Department") officer investigated a criminal operation that sold goods stolen from the nearby railroad yards. [Racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling") targeted Gonzalez as the culprit. After escaping the city, he was trapped along the [Elkhorn River](/wiki/Elkhorn_River "Elkhorn River"), where the mob, including several policemen from Omaha, shot him more than twenty times. It was discovered Gonzalez was unarmed, and he had a reliable alibi for the time of the murder. No one was ever indicted for his killing.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

In the fall of 1919, following [Red Summer](/wiki/Red_Summer "Red Summer"), postwar social and economic tensions, the earlier hiring of African Americans as strikebreakers, and job uncertainty contributed to a mob from South Omaha lynching [Willy Brown](/wiki/Willy_Brown "Willy Brown") and the ensuing [Omaha Race Riot](/wiki/Omaha_Race_Riot "Omaha Race Riot"). Trying to defend Brown, the city's mayor, [Edward Parsons Smith](/wiki/Edward_Parsons_Smith "Edward Parsons Smith"), was lynched also, surviving only after a quick rescue.[[48]](#cite_note-Bristow_2000-49)

Like other industrial cities in the U.S., Omaha suffered severe job losses in the 1950s, more than 10,000 in all, as the railroad and meatpacking industries restructured. Stockyards and packing plants were located closer to ranches, and union achievements were lost as wages declined in surviving jobs.[[156]](#cite_note-160) Many workers left the area if they could get to other jobs. Poverty deepened in areas of the city whose residents depended on those jobs, specifically North and South Omaha. At the same time, with reduced revenues, the city had less financial ability to respond to longstanding problems.

Despair after the April 1968 [assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.") contributed to [riots in North Omaha](/wiki/King_assassination_riots "King assassination riots"), including one at the [Logan Fontenelle Housing Project](/wiki/Logan_Fontenelle_Housing_Project "Logan Fontenelle Housing Project").[[157]](#cite_note-161) For some, the [civil rights movement in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Civil rights movement in Omaha, Nebraska") evolved towards [black nationalism](/wiki/Black_nationalism "Black nationalism"), as the [Black Panther Party](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") was involved in tensions in the late 1960s. Organizations such as the [Black Association for Nationalism Through Unity](/wiki/Black_Association_for_Nationalism_Through_Unity "Black Association for Nationalism Through Unity") became popular among the city's African-American youth. This tension culminated in the *[cause célèbre](/wiki/Cause_c%C3%A9l%C3%A8bre "Cause célèbre")* trial of the [Rice/Poindexter Case](/wiki/Rice/Poindexter_Case "Rice/Poindexter Case"), in which an [Omaha Police Department](/wiki/Omaha_Police_Department "Omaha Police Department") officer was killed by a bomb while answering an emergency call.

Whites in Omaha have followed the [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") pattern, [suburbanizing](/wiki/Suburban "Suburban") to West Omaha.[[158]](#cite_note-162) In the late 1990s and early 2000s, gang violence and incidents between the [Omaha Police](/wiki/Omaha_Police_Department "Omaha Police Department") and Black residents undermined relations between groups in North and South Omaha.[[159]](#cite_note-163)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Economy_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "Economy of Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Omaha%2C_NE_Old_Market_2010.jpg/250px-Omaha%2C_NE_Old_Market_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omaha,_NE_Old_Market_2010.jpg)

[Old Market](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)") in [Downtown Omaha](/wiki/Downtown_Omaha "Downtown Omaha")

With diversification in several industries, including banking, insurance, telecommunications, architecture/construction, and transportation, Omaha's economy has grown since the early 1990s,[[160]](#cite_note-164) and six national fiber optic networks converge in Omaha.[[161]](#cite_note-165)

Omaha's most prominent businessman is Warren Buffett, nicknamed the "Oracle of Omaha", who for decades has ranked as one of the [richest people in the world](/wiki/List_of_billionaires_(2007) "List of billionaires (2007)"). Four Omaha-based companies: [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"), [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad"), [Mutual of Omaha](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha "Mutual of Omaha"), and [Kiewit Corporation](/wiki/Kiewit_Corporation "Kiewit Corporation"), are among the [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500").[[162]](#cite_note-166)

Omaha is the headquarters of several other major corporations, including [the Gallup Organization](/wiki/The_Gallup_Organization "The Gallup Organization"), [Werner Enterprises](/wiki/Werner_Enterprises "Werner Enterprises"), [First National Bank of Omaha](/wiki/First_National_Bank_of_Omaha "First National Bank of Omaha"), [WoodmenLife](/wiki/WoodmenLife "WoodmenLife"), [Gavilon](/wiki/Gavilon "Gavilon"), [Scoular](/wiki/Scoular "Scoular") and First Comp Insurance. Many other large national firms have major operations or operational headquarters in Omaha, including [Bank of the West](/wiki/Bank_of_the_West "Bank of the West"), [First Data](/wiki/First_Data "First Data"), [Sojern](/wiki/Sojern "Sojern"), [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal"), [LinkedIn](/wiki/LinkedIn "LinkedIn"), [Pacific Life](/wiki/Pacific_Life "Pacific Life"), [MetLife](/wiki/MetLife "MetLife") and [Conagra Brands](/wiki/Conagra_Brands "Conagra Brands"). The city is also home to three of the 30 largest architecture firms in the United States, including [HDR, Inc.](/wiki/HDR,_Inc. "HDR, Inc."), [DLR Group, Inc.](/wiki/DLR_Group,_Inc. "DLR Group, Inc."), and [Leo A Daly](/wiki/Leo_A_Daly "Leo A Daly").[[163]](#cite_note-167)
