### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Office_buildings_in_downtown_Omaha.jpg/250px-Office_buildings_in_downtown_Omaha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Office_buildings_in_downtown_Omaha.jpg)

Office buildings in downtown Omaha

Main article: [Tourism in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Tourism_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Tourism in Omaha, Nebraska")

Tourist attractions in Omaha include history, sports, outdoors and cultural experiences. Its principal tourist attractions are the [Henry Doorly Zoo](/wiki/Henry_Doorly_Zoo "Henry Doorly Zoo") and the [College World Series](/wiki/College_World_Series "College World Series").[[165]](#cite_note-Nebraska1-169) The [Old Market](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)") in [Downtown Omaha](/wiki/Downtown_Omaha "Downtown Omaha") is another major attraction and is important to the city's retail economy. Famous early visitors included British author [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") and General [George Crook](/wiki/George_Crook "George Crook"). In 1883 Omaha hosted the first official performance of the [Buffalo Bill](/wiki/Buffalo_Bill "Buffalo Bill")'s [Wild West Show](/wiki/Wild_West_Show "Wild West Show") for 8,000 attendees.[[166]](#cite_note-170) In 1898 the city hosted more than 1 million visitors from across the United States at the [Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition](/wiki/Trans-Mississippi_and_International_Exposition "Trans-Mississippi and International Exposition"), a [world's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") that lasted for more than half the year.[[167]](#cite_note-171)

Research on [leisure](/wiki/Leisure "Leisure") and [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality") situates Omaha in the same tier for tourists as the neighboring cities of [Des Moines, Iowa](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"); [Topeka, Kansas](/wiki/Topeka,_Kansas "Topeka, Kansas"); [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"); [Oklahoma City, Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma_City,_Oklahoma "Oklahoma City, Oklahoma"); [Denver, Colorado](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado"); and [Sioux Falls, South Dakota](/wiki/Sioux_Falls,_South_Dakota "Sioux Falls, South Dakota").[[168]](#cite_note-172) A recent study found investment of $1 million in cultural tourism generated approximately $83,000 in state and local taxes, and provided support for hundreds of jobs for the metropolitan area, which in turn led to additional tax revenue for government.[[165]](#cite_note-Nebraska1-169)[[169]](#cite_note-173)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

Main articles: [Culture of Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Culture_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "Culture of Omaha, Nebraska") and [Culture in North Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Culture_in_North_Omaha,_Nebraska "Culture in North Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Joslyn_Art_Museum_2024.jpg/250px-Joslyn_Art_Museum_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joslyn_Art_Museum_2024.jpg)

Joslyn Art Museum

Several national newspapers, including the *[Boston Globe](/wiki/Boston_Globe "Boston Globe")*[[170]](#cite_note-174) and The *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* [[171]](#cite_note-175) have lauded Omaha's [historical](/wiki/History_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "History of Omaha, Nebraska") and cultural attractions.

The city is home to the [Omaha Community Playhouse](/wiki/Omaha_Community_Playhouse "Omaha Community Playhouse"), the largest [community theater](/wiki/Community_theater "Community theater") in the United States.[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177) The [Omaha Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Omaha_Symphony_Orchestra "Omaha Symphony Orchestra") and its modern [Holland Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Holland_Performing_Arts_Center "Holland Performing Arts Center"),[[174]](#cite_note-178) the [Opera Omaha](/wiki/Opera_Omaha "Opera Omaha") at the [Orpheum](/wiki/Orpheum,_Omaha "Orpheum, Omaha") theater, the [Blue Barn Theatre](/wiki/Blue_Barn_Theatre "Blue Barn Theatre"), [American Midwest Ballet](/wiki/American_Midwest_Ballet "American Midwest Ballet"), and [The Rose Theater](/wiki/The_Rose,_Omaha "The Rose, Omaha") form the backbone of Omaha's [performing arts community](/wiki/Theatre_in_Omaha "Theatre in Omaha"). Opened in 1931, the [Joslyn Art Museum](/wiki/Joslyn_Art_Museum "Joslyn Art Museum") has large art collections.[[175]](#cite_note-179) Since its inception in 1976, [Omaha Children's Museum](/wiki/Omaha_Children%27s_Museum "Omaha Children's Museum") has been a place where children can challenge themselves, discover how the world works and learn through play. The [Bemis Center for Contemporary Arts](/wiki/Bemis_Center_for_Contemporary_Arts "Bemis Center for Contemporary Arts"), one of the nation's premier urban artist colonies, was founded in Omaha in 1981,[[176]](#cite_note-180) and the [Durham Museum](/wiki/Durham_Museum,_Omaha,_Nebraska "Durham Museum, Omaha, Nebraska") is accredited with the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") for traveling exhibits.[[177]](#cite_note-181) The city is also home to the largest singly funded mural in the nation, "Fertile Ground",[[178]](#cite_note-182) by [Meg Saligman](/wiki/Meg_Saligman "Meg Saligman").[[179]](#cite_note-183) The annual [Omaha Blues, Jazz, & Gospel Festival](/wiki/Omaha_Blues,_Jazz,_%26_Gospel_Festival "Omaha Blues, Jazz, & Gospel Festival") celebrates local music along with the [Omaha Black Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Omaha_Black_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Omaha Black Music Hall of Fame").

In 1955, Omaha's [Union Stockyards](/wiki/Union_Stockyards_(Omaha) "Union Stockyards (Omaha)") overtook Chicago's stockyards as the United States' meat packing center. This legacy is reflected in the [cuisine of Omaha](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Omaha "Cuisine of Omaha"), with renowned steakhouses such as [Gorat's](/wiki/Gorat%27s "Gorat's") and the recently closed [Mister C's](/wiki/Mister_C%27s "Mister C's"), as well as the retail chain [Omaha Steaks](/wiki/Omaha_Steaks "Omaha Steaks").

### Henry Doorly Zoo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Henry Doorly Zoo")]

Main article: [Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium](/wiki/Henry_Doorly_Zoo_and_Aquarium "Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Desert_Dome_%285729172276%29.jpg/250px-Desert_Dome_%285729172276%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Desert_Dome_(5729172276).jpg)

Desert Dome at Henry Doorly Zoo and Aquarium

The [Henry Doorly Zoo](/wiki/Henry_Doorly_Zoo "Henry Doorly Zoo") is widely considered a premier zoo.[[180]](#cite_note-184) The zoo is home to the world's largest nocturnal exhibit and indoor swamp; the world's largest indoor rainforest, the world's largest indoor desert,[[181]](#cite_note-185) and the largest [geodesic dome](/wiki/Geodesic_dome "Geodesic dome") in the world (13 stories tall).[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187) The zoo is Nebraska's number-one paid attendance attraction and has welcomed more than 25 million visitors over the past 40 years.[[184]](#cite_note-188)

### Old Market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Old Market")]

Main article: [Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)")

The [Old Market](/wiki/Old_Market_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Old Market (Omaha, Nebraska)") is a major [historic district](/wiki/Historic_district "Historic district") in Downtown Omaha listed on the National Register of Historical Places. Today, its warehouses and other buildings house shops, restaurants, bars, coffee shops, and art galleries.[[185]](#cite_note-189) Downtown is also the location of the Omaha Rail and Commerce Historic District, which has several art galleries and restaurants. [Lauritzen Gardens](/wiki/Lauritzen_Gardens "Lauritzen Gardens") features 100 acres (40 [ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")) with a variety of landscaping, and the new [Kenefick Park](/wiki/Kenefick_Park "Kenefick Park") recognizes Union Pacific Railroad's long history in Omaha.[[186]](#cite_note-190) North Omaha has several historical [cultural](/wiki/Culture_in_North_Omaha,_Nebraska "Culture in North Omaha, Nebraska") attractions including the [Dreamland Historical Project](/wiki/Jewell_Building "Jewell Building"), Love's Jazz and Art Center, and the John Beasley Theater.[[187]](#cite_note-191) The annual River City Roundup is celebrated at Fort Omaha, and the neighborhood of [Florence](/wiki/Florence,_Nebraska "Florence, Nebraska") celebrates its history during "Florence Days". [Native Omaha Days](/wiki/Native_Omaha_Days "Native Omaha Days") is a biennial event celebrating Near North Side heritage.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religious institutions reflect the city's heritage.[[189]](#cite_note-193) The [city's Christian community](/wiki/Christianity_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Christianity in Omaha, Nebraska") has several historical churches dating from the founding of the city. There are also all sizes of congregations, including small, medium and [megachurches](/wiki/Megachurch "Megachurch"). Omaha hosts the only Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints [temple in Nebraska](/wiki/Winter_Quarters_Nebraska_Temple "Winter Quarters Nebraska Temple") along with a large [Jewish community](/wiki/Jews_and_Judaism_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Jews and Judaism in Omaha, Nebraska"). There are 152 parishes in the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Omaha](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Omaha "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Omaha"), and several [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") congregations throughout the city.[[190]](#cite_note-194)

### Lauritzen Gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Lauritzen Gardens")]

[Lauritzen Gardens](/wiki/Lauritzen_Gardens "Lauritzen Gardens") is a [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden") located near [South Omaha](/wiki/South_Omaha "South Omaha"). The garden contains several large [greenhouses](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse"), and outdoor plant exhibits. Covering over 100 acres of land, Lauritzen Gardens welcomes over 200,000 guests annually, making it one of the most popular attractions in Omaha.[[191]](#cite_note-195)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Omaha](/wiki/Music_of_Omaha "Music of Omaha")

Omaha's rich history in rhythm and blues, and jazz gave rise to a number of influential bands, including [Anna Mae Winburn](/wiki/Anna_Mae_Winburn "Anna Mae Winburn")'s [Cotton Club Boys](/wiki/Cotton_Club_Boys_(territory_band) "Cotton Club Boys (territory band)") and [Lloyd Hunter](/wiki/Lloyd_Hunter "Lloyd Hunter")'s Seranaders. [Rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") pioneer [Wynonie Harris](/wiki/Wynonie_Harris "Wynonie Harris"), jazz great [Preston Love](/wiki/Preston_Love "Preston Love"), drummer [Buddy Miles](/wiki/Buddy_Miles "Buddy Miles"), and [Luigi Waites](/wiki/Luigi_Waites "Luigi Waites") are among the city's homegrown talent. [Doug Ingle](/wiki/Doug_Ingle "Doug Ingle") from the late 1960s band [Iron Butterfly](/wiki/Iron_Butterfly "Iron Butterfly") was born in Omaha, as was [indie folk](/wiki/Indie_folk "Indie folk") singer-songwriter [Elliott Smith](/wiki/Elliott_Smith "Elliott Smith"), though both were raised elsewhere. [Musical theater](/wiki/Musical_theater "Musical theater") star [Andrew Rannells](/wiki/Andrew_Rannells "Andrew Rannells") was also born in Omaha and is known amongst his fans for mentioning it in most of his interviews.

Today, the diverse [culture of Omaha](/wiki/Culture_of_Omaha,_Nebraska "Culture of Omaha, Nebraska") includes a variety of performance venues, museums, and musical heritage, including the historically significant jazz scene in North Omaha and the modern and influential "[Omaha Sound](/wiki/Omaha_Sound "Omaha Sound")".[[192]](#cite_note-OPS-196)[[193]](#cite_note-Otto_2010-197)

Contemporary music groups either in or originally from Omaha include [Mannheim Steamroller](/wiki/Mannheim_Steamroller "Mannheim Steamroller"), [Bright Eyes](/wiki/Bright_Eyes_(band) "Bright Eyes (band)"), [The Faint](/wiki/The_Faint "The Faint"), [Cursive](/wiki/Cursive_(band) "Cursive (band)"), [Azure Ray](/wiki/Azure_Ray "Azure Ray"), [Tilly and the Wall](/wiki/Tilly_and_the_Wall "Tilly and the Wall"), and [311](/wiki/311_(band) "311 (band)"). During the late 1990s, Omaha became nationally known as the birthplace of [Saddle Creek Records](/wiki/Saddle_Creek_Records "Saddle Creek Records"), and the subsequent "Omaha Sound" was born from their bands' collective style.[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199)

Omaha also has a fledgling [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") scene. Long-time bastion [Houston Alexander](/wiki/Houston_Alexander "Houston Alexander"), a one-time graffiti artist and professional [Mixed Martial Arts](/wiki/Mixed_Martial_Arts "Mixed Martial Arts") competitor, is a local hip-hop radio show host.[[196]](#cite_note-200)[[197]](#cite_note-201) Cerone Thompson, known as "Scrybe", has had a number one single on college radio stations across the United States. He has also had several number one hits on the local hip hop station respectively titled, "Lose Control" and "Do What U Do".[[198]](#cite_note-media.www.unogateway.com-202) Other notable artists include Stylo of Mastered Trax Latino who holds a strong following in South Omaha and Mexico / Latin America.[[198]](#cite_note-media.www.unogateway.com-202)

Many ethnic and cultural bands have come from Omaha. The [Omaha Black Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Omaha_Black_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Omaha Black Music Hall of Fame") celebrates the city's long history of African-American music and the Strathdon Caledonia Pipe Band carries on a [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") legacy. Internationally renowned composer [Antonín Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák") wrote his [Ninth ("New World") Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._9_(Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k) "Symphony No. 9 (Dvořák)") in 1893 based on his impressions of the region after visiting Omaha's robust [Czech community](/wiki/Czechs_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Czechs in Omaha, Nebraska").[[199]](#cite_note-203) In the period surrounding World War I [Valentin J. Peter](/wiki/Valentin_J._Peter "Valentin J. Peter") encouraged [Germans in Omaha](/wiki/Germans_in_Omaha "Germans in Omaha") to celebrate their rich musical heritage, too. [Frederick Metz](/wiki/Frederick_Metz "Frederick Metz"), [Gottlieb Storz](/wiki/Gottlieb_Storz "Gottlieb Storz") and [Frederick Krug](/wiki/Frederick_Krug "Frederick Krug") were influential brewers whose [beer gardens](/wiki/Beer_garden "Beer garden") kept many German bands active.

### Landmark preservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Landmark preservation")]

Main articles: [Landmarks in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Landmarks_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Landmarks in Omaha, Nebraska") and [List of Registered Historic Places in Douglas County, Nebraska](/wiki/List_of_Registered_Historic_Places_in_Douglas_County,_Nebraska "List of Registered Historic Places in Douglas County, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/091407-JoslynCastle.jpg/250px-091407-JoslynCastle.jpg)](/wiki/File:091407-JoslynCastle.jpg)

[Joslyn Castle](/wiki/Joslyn_Castle "Joslyn Castle")

Omaha is home to dozens of nationally, regionally and locally significant landmarks.[[200]](#cite_note-204) The city has more than a dozen [historic districts](/wiki/Historic_district "Historic district"), including [Fort Omaha Historic District](/wiki/Fort_Omaha_Historic_District "Fort Omaha Historic District"), [Gold Coast Historic District](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Historic_District_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Gold Coast Historic District (Omaha, Nebraska)"), [Omaha Quartermaster Depot Historic District](/wiki/Omaha_Quartermaster_Depot_Historic_District "Omaha Quartermaster Depot Historic District"), Field Club Historic District, Bemis Park Historic District, and the South Omaha Main Street Historic District. Omaha is notorious for its 1989 demolition of 24 buildings in the Jobbers Canyon Historic District, which represents to date the largest loss of buildings on the National Register.[[201]](#cite_note-205) The only original building surviving of that complex is the [Nash Block](/wiki/Nash_Block "Nash Block").

Omaha has almost [one hundred individual properties](/wiki/List_of_Registered_Historic_Places_in_Douglas_County,_Nebraska "List of Registered Historic Places in Douglas County, Nebraska") listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"), including the [Bank of Florence](/wiki/Bank_of_Florence "Bank of Florence"), [Holy Family Church](/wiki/Holy_Family_Catholic_Church_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Holy Family Catholic Church (Omaha, Nebraska)"), the [Christian Specht Building](/wiki/Christian_Specht_Building "Christian Specht Building") and the [Joslyn Castle](/wiki/Joslyn_Castle "Joslyn Castle"). There are also three properties designated as [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").[[202]](#cite_note-206)

[Locally designated landmarks](/wiki/Omaha_Landmarks "Omaha Landmarks"), including residential, commercial, religious, educational, agricultural and socially significant locations across the city, honor Omaha's cultural legacy and important history. The [City of Omaha](/wiki/Government_of_Omaha "Government of Omaha") [Landmarks Heritage Preservation Commission](/wiki/Landmarks_Heritage_Preservation_Commission "Landmarks Heritage Preservation Commission") is the government body that works with the [mayor of Omaha](/wiki/Mayor_of_Omaha "Mayor of Omaha") and the [Omaha City Council](/wiki/Omaha_City_Council "Omaha City Council") to protect historic places. Important history organizations in the community include the [Douglas County Historical Society](/wiki/Douglas_County_Historical_Society "Douglas County Historical Society").[[203]](#cite_note-207)

Built in 1962, Omaha's [Cinerama](/wiki/Cinerama "Cinerama") was called [Indian Hills Theater](/wiki/Indian_Hills_Theater "Indian Hills Theater"). Its demolition in 2001 by the [Nebraska Methodist Health System](/wiki/Nebraska_Methodist_Health_System "Nebraska Methodist Health System") was unpopular, with objections from local historical and cultural groups and luminaries from around the world.[[204]](#cite_note-208) The [Dundee Theatre](/wiki/Dundee_Theatre "Dundee Theatre") is the lone surviving single-screen movie theater in Omaha and still shows films.[[205]](#cite_note-209) A recent development to the Omaha film scene was the addition of [Film Streams](/wiki/Film_Streams "Film Streams")'s Ruth Sokolof Theater in North Downtown. The two-screen theater is part of the [Slowdown](/wiki/Slowdown_(venue) "Slowdown (venue)") facility. It features American independents, foreign films, documentaries, classics, themed series, and director retrospectives. In addition to the five [Douglas Theatres](/wiki/Douglas_Theatres "Douglas Theatres") venues in Omaha, two more are opening, including [Midtown Crossing](/wiki/Midtown_Omaha#Neighborhoods "Midtown Omaha") Theatres, on 32nd and Farnam Streets near the [Mutual of Omaha Building](/wiki/Mutual_of_Omaha_Building "Mutual of Omaha Building"). [Westroads Mall](/wiki/Westroads_Mall "Westroads Mall") has a modern multiplex [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") with 14 screens, operated by [Rave Cinemas](/wiki/Rave_Cinemas "Rave Cinemas").[[206]](#cite_note-210)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Sports_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Sports in Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/2019CWSVanderbiltVsLouisville.jpg/250px-2019CWSVanderbiltVsLouisville.jpg)](/wiki/File:2019CWSVanderbiltVsLouisville.jpg)

Charles Schwab Field

Sports have been important in Omaha for more than a century, and the city plays host to three minor-league professional sports teams.

Omaha has hosted the annual June NCAA [College World Series](/wiki/College_World_Series "College World Series") men's baseball tournament since 1950.[[207]](#cite_note-211) It has been played at the downtown [Charles Schwab Field](/wiki/Charles_Schwab_Field "Charles Schwab Field") since 2011.[[208]](#cite_note-212)

The Omaha Sports Commission is a quasi-governmental nonprofit organization that coordinates much of the professional and amateur athletic activity in the city, including the 2008, 2012 and 2016 US Olympic Swimming Team Trials and the building of a [new stadium](/wiki/TD_Ameritrade_Park_Omaha "TD Ameritrade Park Omaha") in North Downtown.[[209]](#cite_note-213)[[210]](#cite_note-214)[[211]](#cite_note-215) The [University of Nebraska–Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") and the Commission co-hosted the 2008 [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) [Division One Women's Volleyball Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Volleyball_Championship "NCAA Women's Volleyball Championship") in December of that year.[[212]](#cite_note-216) The 2016 Big 10 Baseball Championship was also played at the College World Series Stadium. Another quasi-governmental board, the Metropolitan Entertainment and Convention Authority (MECA), was created by city voters in 2000,[[213]](#cite_note-217) and is responsible for maintaining the [CHI Health Center Omaha](/wiki/CHI_Health_Center_Omaha "CHI Health Center Omaha") (formerly CenturyLink Center Omaha).[[214]](#cite_note-218)

The Omaha Storm Chasers play at [Werner Park](/wiki/Werner_Park "Werner Park").[[217]](#cite_note-221) They won seven championships (in 1969, 1970, 1978, 1990, 2011, 2013, and 2014).
Omaha is also home to the Omaha Diamond Spirit, a collegiate summer baseball team that plays in the MINK league.

The [Omaha Supernovas](/wiki/Omaha_Supernovas "Omaha Supernovas") are a professional [indoor volleyball](/wiki/Indoor_volleyball "Indoor volleyball") team based in Omaha, Nebraska. The team competes in the [Pro Volleyball Federation](/wiki/Pro_Volleyball_Federation "Pro Volleyball Federation") (PVF). The Supernovas began play in the league's inaugural [2024 season](/wiki/2024_Pro_Volleyball_Federation_season "2024 Pro Volleyball Federation season"). The team plays their home games at [CHI Health Center Omaha](/wiki/CHI_Health_Center_Omaha "CHI Health Center Omaha"). The Supernovas won the inaugural championship in May 2024.[[218]](#cite_note-222) During its championship run in the inaugural PVF season, Omaha and the Supernovas became the league's shining star, hosting 134,969 fans across the 15 matches held at the CHI Health Center. That includes a whopping 9,656 average mark for the 12 Supernovas' home matches, plus the 19,094 spectators who attended the PVF Semifinals and Championship. The Supernovas erased any doubt that professional volleyball could happen in the United States with their 9,656 match average the No. 1 mark amongst professional volleyball teams in the world.

Omaha broke many of its own attendance records across its historic season, starting with 11,624 fans attending the first-ever PVF match on Jan. 24 between Omaha and the [Atlanta Vibe](/wiki/Atlanta_Vibe "Atlanta Vibe"). That mark was broken a few weeks later on Sunday, Feb. 18 as 11,918 fans showed up to watch the Supernovas take on the Orlando Valkyries. The newest and most current attendance record was set on Saturday, March 16 with 12,090 spectators packing into the CHI Health Center to see the Supernovas beat the [Valkyries](/wiki/Orlando_Valkyries "Orlando Valkyries") in four sets.[[216]](#cite_note-provolleyball.com-220)

[Union Omaha](/wiki/Union_Omaha "Union Omaha"), a professional minor league soccer team, is a member of [USL League One](/wiki/USL_League_One "USL League One") and began play in the [2020 season](/wiki/2020_USL_League_One_season "2020 USL League One season"). Their home games are played at Werner Park, which it shares with the Storm Chasers.[[219]](#cite_note-223) The team, nicknamed the Owls, won the league championship in 2021.[[220]](#cite_note-224) Union then made a deep run to the quarterfinals of the [2022 U.S. Open Cup](/wiki/2022_U.S._Open_Cup "2022 U.S. Open Cup"), defeating two [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") teams in the process.[[221]](#cite_note-225) The team announced plans in 2024 to build a 7,000-seat [soccer-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") near Downtown Omaha.[[222]](#cite_note-226)

The [Creighton University](/wiki/Creighton_University "Creighton University") [Bluejays](/wiki/Creighton_Bluejays "Creighton Bluejays") compete in a number of [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") sports as members of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference"). The Bluejays play [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") at [Charles Schwab Field](/wiki/Charles_Schwab_Field "Charles Schwab Field"), [soccer](/wiki/College_soccer_in_the_United_States "College soccer in the United States") at [Morrison Stadium](/wiki/Morrison_Stadium "Morrison Stadium"), and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") at the 18,000 seat [CHI Health Center Omaha](/wiki/CHI_Health_Center_Omaha "CHI Health Center Omaha"). The Jays annually rank in the top 15 in attendance each year, averaging more than 16,000 people per game.
The [Omaha Mavericks](/wiki/Omaha_Mavericks "Omaha Mavericks"), representing the [University of Nebraska Omaha](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska_Omaha "University of Nebraska Omaha") (UNO), also play basketball, baseball and soccer in NCAA Division I as members of [The Summit League](/wiki/The_Summit_League "The Summit League"). The UNO [men's ice hockey team](/wiki/Omaha_Mavericks_men%27s_ice_hockey "Omaha Mavericks men's ice hockey") plays in the [National Collegiate Hockey Conference](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Hockey_Conference "National Collegiate Hockey Conference").

[Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") is a popular spectator sport in Omaha. The [Omaha Lancers](/wiki/Omaha_Lancers "Omaha Lancers"), a [United States Hockey League](/wiki/United_States_Hockey_League "United States Hockey League") team, play at the Ralston Arena.[[223]](#cite_note-227) The Omaha Mavericks play in the on-campus [Baxter Arena](/wiki/Baxter_Arena "Baxter Arena").

Omaha was home to an [expansion team](/wiki/UFL_Omaha "UFL Omaha"), the Nighthawks, in the [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2009) "United Football League (2009)") from 2010 to 2011.[[224]](#cite_note-228) The [Omaha Beef](/wiki/Omaha_Beef "Omaha Beef") [indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") team played at the [Omaha Civic Auditorium](/wiki/Omaha_Civic_Auditorium "Omaha Civic Auditorium") until 2012 when they moved to the new [Ralston Arena](/wiki/Ralston_Arena "Ralston Arena").

Omaha was a notable cadence term of [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") quarterback [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") during his 18-year playing career used to indicate a change of playcall.[[225]](#cite_note-229) In 2021, he launched [Omaha Productions](/wiki/Omaha_Productions "Omaha Productions").

The [Kansas City-Omaha Kings](/wiki/Kansas_City-Omaha_Kings "Kansas City-Omaha Kings"), an [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA") franchise, played in both cities from 1972 to 1978,[[226]](#cite_note-230) before decamping solely to Kansas City until 1985, when the team moved to its current home of [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento "Sacramento").

The [Cox Classic](/wiki/Cox_Classic "Cox Classic") golf tournament was part of the [Web.com Tour](/wiki/Web.com_Tour "Web.com Tour") from 1996 to 2013. The circuit returned to Omaha in 2017 with the [Pinnacle Bank Championship](/wiki/Pinnacle_Bank_Championship "Pinnacle Bank Championship").

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Main articles: [Parks in Omaha](/wiki/Parks_in_Omaha "Parks in Omaha") and [Trails in Omaha](/wiki/Trails_in_Omaha "Trails in Omaha")

Omaha has a thriving running community and many miles of paved running and biking trails throughout the city and surrounding communities. The Omaha Marathon involves a half-marathon and a 10 km (6.2 mi) race that takes place annually in September.[[227]](#cite_note-231) Omaha also has a history of [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling"), including multiple junior national champions.[[228]](#cite_note-232)
The city's historic [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevards_in_Omaha "Boulevards in Omaha") were originally designed by [Horace Cleveland](/wiki/Horace_Cleveland "Horace Cleveland") in 1889 to work with the parks to create a seamless flow of trees, grass and flowers throughout the city. [Florence Boulevard](/wiki/Florence_Boulevard "Florence Boulevard") and Fontenelle Boulevard are among the remnants of this system.[[229]](#cite_note-233) Omaha boasts more than 80 mi (129 km) of [trails](/wiki/Trails_in_Omaha "Trails in Omaha") for [pedestrians](/wiki/Pedestrian "Pedestrian"), [bicyclists](/wiki/Bicyclist "Bicyclist") and [hikers](/wiki/Hiker "Hiker").[[230]](#cite_note-234) They include the [American Discovery Trail](/wiki/American_Discovery_Trail "American Discovery Trail"), which traverses the entire United States, and the [Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_National_Historic_Trail "Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail") passes through Omaha as it travels 3,700 mi (5,950 km) westward from Illinois to Oregon. Trails throughout the area are included in comprehensive plans for the city of Omaha, the Omaha metropolitan area, Douglas County, and long-distance coordinated plans between the municipalities of southeast Nebraska. The city also has a park dedicated to pollinating bees and insects called 'Pacific Preserve'[[231]](#cite_note-235)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of Omaha](/wiki/Government_of_Omaha "Government of Omaha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Omaha_NE_city_building.jpg/250px-Omaha_NE_city_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omaha_NE_city_building.jpg)

City Building in Downtown Omaha

Omaha has a [strong mayor](/wiki/Mayor-council_government "Mayor-council government") form of [government](/wiki/Government "Government"), along with a city council elected from seven districts across the city. The [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Omaha "Mayor of Omaha") is [John Ewing Jr.](/wiki/John_Ewing_Jr. "John Ewing Jr."), who was elected in May 2025. The longest-serving mayor in Omaha's history was ["Cowboy" Jim Dahlman](/wiki/James_Dahlman "James Dahlman"), who served 20 years over eight terms. He was regarded as the "wettest mayor in America" because of the flourishing number of bars in Omaha during his tenure.[[232]](#cite_note-236) Dahlman was a close associate of [political boss](/wiki/Political_boss "Political boss") Tom Dennison.[[233]](#cite_note-237) During Dahlman's tenure, the city switched from its original strong-mayor form of government to a [city commission government](/wiki/City_commission_government "City commission government").[[234]](#cite_note-238) In 1956, the city switched back.[[235]](#cite_note-239)

The [city clerk](/wiki/City_clerk "City clerk") is Elizabeth Butler.[[236]](#cite_note-240) The City of Omaha administers twelve departments, including finance, [police](/wiki/Omaha_Police_Department "Omaha Police Department"), human rights, [libraries](/wiki/Omaha_Public_Library "Omaha Public Library") and planning.[[237]](#cite_note-241) The Omaha City Council is the legislative branch and has seven members elected from districts across the city. The council enacts [local ordinances](/wiki/Local_ordinance "Local ordinance") and approves the city [budget](/wiki/Budget "Budget"). Government priorities and activities are established in a budget ordinance approved annually. The council takes official action through the passage of ordinances and resolutions. Nebraska's constitution grants the option of [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") to cities with more than 5,000 residents, meaning they may operate under their own charters. Omaha is one of only three cities in Nebraska to use this option, out of 17 eligible.[[238]](#cite_note-242) The City of Omaha is considering [consolidating](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county") with Douglas County government.[[239]](#cite_note-243)

Although registered [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") outnumbered [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in the [2nd congressional district](/wiki/Nebraska%27s_2nd_congressional_district "Nebraska's 2nd congressional district"), which includes Omaha, Democratic presidential candidate [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") opened three campaign offices in the city with 15 staff members to cover the state in fall 2008.[[240]](#cite_note-244) Mike Fahey, the Democratic mayor of Omaha, said he would do whatever it took to deliver the district's electoral vote to Obama; and the [Obama campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign") considered the district "in play".[[241]](#cite_note-245) Former Nebraska [U.S. Senator](/wiki/U.S._Senator "U.S. Senator") [Bob Kerrey](/wiki/Bob_Kerrey "Bob Kerrey") and then-U.S. Senator [Ben Nelson](/wiki/Ben_Nelson "Ben Nelson") campaigned in the city for Obama,[[242]](#cite_note-246) and in November 2008 Obama won the district's electoral vote. This was an historical win, as Obama became the first Democratic presidential candidate to win an electoral vote in Nebraska since 1964, only made possible by [Nebraska's split electoral vote system.](/wiki/Congressional_District_Method "Congressional District Method")[[243]](#cite_note-247)

In 2011, Nebraska lawmakers moved Offutt Air Force Base and the town of Bellevue—an area with a large minority population—out of the Omaha-based 2nd district and shifted in the Republican-heavy Omaha suburbs in Sarpy County. The move is expected to dilute the city's urban Democratic vote.[[244]](#cite_note-2nd-248)

The 2nd district sent its single electoral vote for [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in the 2020 election.[[245]](#cite_note-249) Biden's victory, by more than 20,000 votes, shows Omaha's and the 2nd district's continuing trend toward Democratic politics in recent years.[[246]](#cite_note-250)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Education_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Education in Omaha, Nebraska")

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Omaha has many public and private educational institutions, including [Omaha Public Schools](/wiki/Omaha_Public_Schools "Omaha Public Schools"), the largest [public](/wiki/Public_school_(government_funded) "Public school (government funded)") [school district](/wiki/School_district "School district") in Nebraska, which serves more than 47,750 students in more than 75 schools.[[247]](#cite_note-251) After a contentious period of uncertainty, in 2007 the [Nebraska Legislature](/wiki/Nebraska_Legislature "Nebraska Legislature") approved a plan to create a [learning community](/wiki/Learning_community "Learning community") for Omaha-area school districts with a central administrative board.[[248]](#cite_note-252)

The [Westside Community Schools](/wiki/Westside_Community_Schools "Westside Community Schools"), also known as District 66, is a district in the heart of Omaha. It serves students in pre-kindergarten through the 12th grade and recorded a district enrollment of 6,123 students K-12 for the 2015–16 school year.[[249]](#cite_note-253)
Through annexations Omaha also has the [Millard Public Schools](/wiki/Millard_Public_Schools "Millard Public Schools") and [Elkhorn Public Schools](/wiki/Elkhorn_Public_Schools "Elkhorn Public Schools"). Omaha is also home to [Brownell-Talbot School](/wiki/Brownell-Talbot_School "Brownell-Talbot School"), Nebraska's only preschool through grade 12, independent college preparatory school.

The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Omaha](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Omaha "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Omaha") operates [numerous private Catholic schools](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Omaha#Omaha_Catholic_schools "Archdiocese of Omaha") with 21,500 students in 32 elementary schools and nine high schools.[[250]](#cite_note-254) They include [St. Cecilia Grade School](https://web.archive.org/web/20120618145254/http://stcecilia.omaha.org/) in Midtown Omaha, Holy Cross in Morton Meadows, St. Robert Bellarmine School at 120th and Pacific Street, St. Stephen the Martyr School in Millard, and Creighton Preparatory School, all of which have received the [U.S. Department of Education](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Education "U.S. Department of Education") [Blue Ribbon School](/wiki/Blue_Ribbon_School "Blue Ribbon School") award.

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Higher education")]

There are eleven [colleges and universities](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of colleges and universities in Omaha, Nebraska") among Omaha's [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") institutions. The largest public school is [University of Nebraska Omaha](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska_Omaha "University of Nebraska Omaha"), which was founded in 1908 and is currently an NCAA Division I school with over 15,000 students. The [University of Nebraska Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska_Medical_Center "University of Nebraska Medical Center") in midtown Omaha is home to the [Eppley Cancer Center](/wiki/Eppley_Cancer_Center "Eppley Cancer Center"), one of 66 designated Cancer Centers by the [National Cancer Institute](/wiki/National_Cancer_Institute "National Cancer Institute") in the United States. The University of Nebraska College of Medicine is also on the UNMC campus.

Omaha's largest private university is [Creighton University](/wiki/Creighton_University "Creighton University"). It is a Jesuit institution that is ranked the top non-doctoral regional university in the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"). Its campus is just outside Downtown Omaha in the new North Downtown district. The university has a combined 6,700 students in its undergraduate, graduate, medical, and law schools.

Omaha is also home to a number of smaller colleges and universities. [Clarkson College](/wiki/Clarkson_College "Clarkson College") is a small private college focusing on health sciences and affiliated with the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"). [Nebraska Methodist College](/wiki/Nebraska_Methodist_College "Nebraska Methodist College") is a small private school focusing on health careers and education. The [College of Saint Mary](/wiki/College_of_Saint_Mary "College of Saint Mary") is a Catholic woman's school known for its healthcare offerings. [Bellevue University](/wiki/Bellevue_University "Bellevue University") is a mid-size private university. [Doane University](/wiki/Doane_University "Doane University") is the oldest private university in Nebraska, with campuses in Crete and Lincoln. Its residential campus is in Crete with a smaller campus in Omaha.

Further information: [Capitol School of Hairstyling & Esthetics](/wiki/Capitol_School_of_Hairstyling_%26_Esthetics "Capitol School of Hairstyling & Esthetics")

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/Media_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Media in Omaha, Nebraska")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/NE_Corner_view_of_Omaha_Star_Building.jpg/250px-NE_Corner_view_of_Omaha_Star_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:NE_Corner_view_of_Omaha_Star_Building.jpg)

The [Omaha Star building](/wiki/Omaha_Star_building "Omaha Star building") is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Douglas_County,_Nebraska "National Register of Historic Places listings in Douglas County, Nebraska").

The city is the focus of the Omaha [designated market area](/wiki/Designated_market_area "Designated market area"), and is the 76th largest in the United States.[[251]](#cite_note-255)

### Magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Magazines")]

*Omaha Magazine*[[252]](#cite_note-256)

### Newspapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Newspapers")]

The major daily newspaper in Nebraska is the *[Omaha World-Herald](/wiki/Omaha_World-Herald "Omaha World-Herald")*, formerly the largest employee-owned newspaper in the United States.[[253]](#cite_note-257) Weeklies in the city include the Midlands Business Journal (weekly business publication); *American Classifieds* (formerly *Thrifty Nickel*), a weekly classified newspaper; *[The Reader](/wiki/The_Reader_(newspaper) "The Reader (newspaper)")*, as well as *[The Omaha Star](/wiki/The_Omaha_Star "The Omaha Star")*. Founded in 1938 in North Omaha, the *Star* is Nebraska's only African-American newspaper.[[254]](#cite_note-258)

### Television stations and cable TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Television stations and cable TV")]

Omaha's three television news stations include: [KETV](/wiki/KETV "KETV") 7 (ABC- branded NewsWatch 7), [KMTV-TV](/wiki/KMTV-TV "KMTV-TV") 3 (CBS- branded 3 News Now), and [WOWT](/wiki/WOWT "WOWT") 6 (NBC Omaha). [KPTM](/wiki/KPTM "KPTM") 42 (FOX 42/CW 15) and [KXVO](/wiki/KXVO "KXVO") 15 ([TBD](/wiki/TBD_(TV_network) "TBD (TV network)")) do not air local news content. [Cox Communications](/wiki/Cox_Communications "Cox Communications") provides cable television services throughout the metropolitan area.[[255]](#cite_note-259) [Prism TV](/wiki/Prism_TV "Prism TV"), offered through [CenturyLink](/wiki/CenturyLink "CenturyLink"), is a broadband TV option also available throughout the Omaha area. Satellite providers such as [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV") and [Dish Network](/wiki/Dish_Network "Dish Network") and the local programming they offer are also available throughout the metropolitan area.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Further information: [List of tallest buildings in Omaha, Nebraska](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "List of tallest buildings in Omaha, Nebraska")

Omaha's growth has required the constant development of new urban [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") that influence, allow and encourage the constant expansion of the city.

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Retail [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas_utility "Natural gas utility") and [water](/wiki/Water_company "Water company") [public utilities](/wiki/Public_utilities "Public utilities") in Omaha are provided by the [Metropolitan Utilities District](/wiki/Metropolitan_Utilities_District "Metropolitan Utilities District").[[256]](#cite_note-260) Nebraska is the only public power state in the nation. All electric utilities are non-profit and customer-owned. [Electricity](/wiki/Electrical_power_industry "Electrical power industry") in the city is provided by the [Omaha Public Power District](/wiki/Omaha_Public_Power_District "Omaha Public Power District").[[257]](#cite_note-261) [Public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing") is governed by the [Omaha Housing Authority](/wiki/Omaha_Housing_Authority "Omaha Housing Authority"). [Metro Area Transit](/wiki/Metro_Area_Transit "Metro Area Transit") provides [public transportation](/wiki/Public_transportation "Public transportation"). [CenturyLink](/wiki/CenturyLink "CenturyLink") and [Cox](/wiki/Cox_Communications "Cox Communications") provide local telephone and internet services. The City of Omaha maintains two modern [sewage treatment plants](/wiki/Sewage_treatment_plant "Sewage treatment plant").[[258]](#cite_note-262)

Portions of the [Enron](/wiki/Enron "Enron") corporation began as [Northern Natural Gas Company](/wiki/Northern_Natural_Gas_Company "Northern Natural Gas Company") in Omaha. Northern provides three natural gas lines to Omaha. Enron formerly owned UtiliCorp United, Inc., which became [Aquila, Inc.](/wiki/Aquila,_Inc. "Aquila, Inc.") Peoples Natural Gas, a division of Aquila, Inc., serves several surrounding communities around the Omaha metropolitan area, including [Plattsmouth](/wiki/Plattsmouth "Plattsmouth").[[259]](#cite_note-263)

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Health care")]

There are [several hospitals in Omaha](/wiki/Hospitals_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Hospitals in Omaha, Nebraska"). Research hospitals include the Boys Town National Research Hospital, the [University of Nebraska Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska_Medical_Center "University of Nebraska Medical Center") and the [Creighton University Medical Center](/wiki/Creighton_University_Medical_Center "Creighton University Medical Center"). The Boys Town facility is well known for hearing-related research and treatment. The University of Nebraska Medical Center hosts the [Eppley Institute for Research in Cancer and Allied Diseases](/wiki/Eppley_Institute_for_Research_in_Cancer_and_Allied_Diseases "Eppley Institute for Research in Cancer and Allied Diseases"), a world-renowned [cancer treatment](/wiki/Cancer_treatment "Cancer treatment") facility named in honor of Omahan [Eugene Eppley](/wiki/Eugene_Eppley "Eugene Eppley").[[260]](#cite_note-264)[[261]](#cite_note-265)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Omaha](/wiki/Transportation_in_Omaha "Transportation in Omaha")

Further information: [Railroads in Omaha](/wiki/Railroads_in_Omaha "Railroads in Omaha") and [Trails in Omaha](/wiki/Trails_in_Omaha "Trails in Omaha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Ak-Sar-Ben_Bridge_1938_LOC_11763u.jpg/250px-Ak-Sar-Ben_Bridge_1938_LOC_11763u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ak-Sar-Ben_Bridge_1938_LOC_11763u.jpg)

[Ak-Sar-Ben Bridge](/wiki/Ak-Sar-Ben_Bridge "Ak-Sar-Ben Bridge") toll booth in 1938

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Interstate_leaving_Omaha.jpg/250px-Interstate_leaving_Omaha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interstate_leaving_Omaha.jpg)

Interstate 480 leaving Omaha

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Omaha-eppley.jpg/250px-Omaha-eppley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omaha-eppley.jpg)

Omaha's [Eppley Airfield](/wiki/Eppley_Airfield "Eppley Airfield") in East Omaha

Omaha's central role in the history of transportation across America earned it the nickname "Gate City of the West".[[1]](#cite_note-Mullens,_P.A._1901_p._24-1) Despite [President Lincoln's](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") decree that [Council Bluffs, Iowa](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"), be the starting point for the Union Pacific Railroad, construction began from Omaha on the eastern portion of the first transcontinental railroad.[[262]](#cite_note-266) By the middle of the 20th century, nearly every major railroad served Omaha.

Today, the Omaha Rail and Commerce Historic District celebrates this connection, along with the listing of the [Burlington Train Station](/wiki/Burlington_Station_(Omaha,_Nebraska) "Burlington Station (Omaha, Nebraska)") and the [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Omaha) "Union Station (Omaha)") on the National Register of Historic Places. First housed in the former [Herndon House](/wiki/Herndon_House "Herndon House"), the Union Pacific Railroad's corporate headquarters have been in Omaha since the company began.[[263]](#cite_note-267) Their new headquarters, the [Union Pacific Center](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Center "Union Pacific Center"), opened in Downtown Omaha in 2004.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), the national passenger rail system, provides service through Omaha, with the [California Zephyr](/wiki/California_Zephyr "California Zephyr") serving [Omaha station](/wiki/Omaha_station "Omaha station") once daily in each direction. The [intercity bus terminal](/wiki/Omaha_Bus_Station "Omaha Bus Station") is at 1601 Jackson St. in downtown Omaha. The terminal also service to [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines"), [Burlington Trailways](/wiki/Burlington_Trailways "Burlington Trailways"), and [Express Arrow](/w/index.php?title=Express_Arrow&action=edit&redlink=1 "Express Arrow (page does not exist)"). [Metro Transit](/wiki/Metro_Area_Transit "Metro Area Transit"), previously known as Metro Area Transit, is the [local bus system](/wiki/Mass_transportation "Mass transportation").

Omaha's position as a transportation center was finalized with the 1872 opening of the [Union Pacific Missouri River Bridge](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Missouri_River_Bridge "Union Pacific Missouri River Bridge") that linked the transcontinental railroad to the railroads terminating in Council Bluffs.[[264]](#cite_note-268) In 1888, the first road bridge, the [Douglas Street Bridge](/wiki/Douglas_Street_Bridge "Douglas Street Bridge"), opened. In the 1890s, the [Illinois Central](/wiki/Illinois_Central "Illinois Central") drawbridge opened as the largest bridge of its type in the world. Omaha's Missouri River road bridges are now entering their second generation, including the [Works Progress Administration](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration")-financed [South Omaha Bridge](/wiki/South_Omaha_Bridge "South Omaha Bridge"), now called Veteran's Memorial Bridge, which was added to the National Register of Historic Places. In 2006, Omaha and Council Bluffs announced joint plans to build the [Missouri River Pedestrian Bridge](/wiki/Missouri_River_Pedestrian_Bridge "Missouri River Pedestrian Bridge"), which opened in 2008.[[265]](#cite_note-269)

The primary mode of transportation in Omaha is by automobile, with [I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Nebraska "Interstate 80 in Nebraska"), [I-480](/wiki/Interstate_480_(Iowa-Nebraska) "Interstate 480 (Iowa-Nebraska)"), [I-680](/wiki/Interstate_680_(Iowa-Nebraska) "Interstate 680 (Iowa-Nebraska)"), [I-29](/wiki/Interstate_29 "Interstate 29"), and [U.S. Route 75](/wiki/U.S._Route_75 "U.S. Route 75") (JFK Freeway and North Freeway) providing [freeway](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway") service across the metropolitan area.[[266]](#cite_note-270) The expressway along West Dodge Road ([U.S. Route 6](/wiki/U.S._Route_6 "U.S. Route 6") and [Nebraska Link 28B](/wiki/Nebraska_Link_28B "Nebraska Link 28B")) and [U.S. Route 275](/wiki/U.S._Route_275 "U.S. Route 275") has been upgraded to freeway standards from I-680 to [Fremont](/wiki/Fremont,_Nebraska "Fremont, Nebraska"). City-owned [Metro Transit](/wiki/Metro_Area_Transit "Metro Area Transit"), formerly MAT Metro Area Transit, provides public bus service to hundreds of locations throughout the Metro.

A 2017 study by [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") ranked Omaha 26th most walkable of fifty largest U.S. cities.[[267]](#cite_note-271) Of the top 50 most walkable cities only one, Omaha, Nebraska, saw its [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") decline, and it only decreased 0.3 points from last year.[[268]](#cite_note-272) There is an extensive [trail system](/wiki/Trails_in_Omaha,_Nebraska "Trails in Omaha, Nebraska") throughout the city for walkers, runners, bicyclists, and other pedestrian modes of transportation.

Omaha is laid out on a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), with 12 blocks to the mile with a north-to-south [house numbering](/wiki/House_numbering "House numbering") system.[[269]](#cite_note-273) Omaha is the location of a [historic boulevard system](/wiki/Boulevards_in_Omaha "Boulevards in Omaha") designed by [H.W.S. Cleveland](/wiki/H.W.S._Cleveland "H.W.S. Cleveland") who sought to combine the beauty of parks with the pleasure of driving cars.[[270]](#cite_note-274) The historic [Florence](/wiki/Florence_Boulevard "Florence Boulevard") and Fontenelle Boulevards, as well as the modern [Sorenson Parkway](/w/index.php?title=Sorenson_Parkway&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sorenson Parkway (page does not exist)"), are important elements in this system.[[271]](#cite_note-275) The City of Omaha has proposed the [Omaha Streetcar](/wiki/Omaha_Streetcar "Omaha Streetcar") through the city's urban core, with proposed extensions to [Council Bluffs, Iowa](/wiki/Council_Bluffs,_Iowa "Council Bluffs, Iowa"), [Eppley Airfield](/wiki/Eppley_Airfield "Eppley Airfield"), [North Omaha](/wiki/North_Omaha "North Omaha"), [West Omaha](/wiki/West_Omaha "West Omaha"), and [Bellevue, Nebraska](/wiki/Bellevue,_Nebraska "Bellevue, Nebraska").[[272]](#cite_note-GaWC-276)

[Eppley Airfield](/wiki/Eppley_Airfield "Eppley Airfield"), Omaha's airport, serves the region with over 5 million passengers in 2018.[[273]](#cite_note-277) [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines"), [Southwest Airlines](/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines"), [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines"), [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"), [Alaska Airlines](/wiki/Alaska_Airlines "Alaska Airlines"), [Allegiant Air](/wiki/Allegiant_Air "Allegiant Air"), [Frontier Airlines](/wiki/Frontier_Airlines "Frontier Airlines"), and [Sun Country Airlines](/wiki/Sun_Country_Airlines "Sun Country Airlines") serve the airport with direct and connecting service. As of 2018, the airport has non-stop service to 34 destinations. [General aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airports that serve the area include the Millard Municipal Airport, [North Omaha Airport](/wiki/North_Omaha_Airport "North Omaha Airport") and the [Council Bluffs Airport](/wiki/Council_Bluffs_Airport "Council Bluffs Airport"). Offutt Air Force Base continues to serve as a military airbase; it is at the southern edge of Bellevue, which in turn lies immediately south of Omaha.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Omaha,_Nebraska&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* The 1935 winner of the [Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing") was named [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha_(horse) "Omaha (horse)").
* Omaha's [Boys Town](/wiki/Boys_Town_(organization) "Boys Town (organization)") was made famous by the 1938 [Spencer Tracy](/wiki/Spencer_Tracy "Spencer Tracy") and [Mickey Rooney](/wiki/Mickey_Rooney "Mickey Rooney") movie *[Boys Town](/wiki/Boys_Town_(film) "Boys Town (film)")*.
* In 1939, Omaha hosted the world premiere of the film *[Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific_(film) "Union Pacific (film)")* and the accompanying three-day celebration drew 250,000 people. A special train from [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") carried director [Cecil B. DeMille](/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille "Cecil B. DeMille") and stars [Barbara Stanwyck](/wiki/Barbara_Stanwyck "Barbara Stanwyck") and [Joel McCrea](/wiki/Joel_McCrea "Joel McCrea").[[274]](#cite_note-278)
* The city has been featured in several films by Omaha native, [Alexander Payne](/wiki/Alexander_Payne "Alexander Payne"), including *[Citizen Ruth](/wiki/Citizen_Ruth "Citizen Ruth")* (1996), *[Election](/wiki/Election_(1999_film) "Election (1999 film)")* (1999) and *[About Schmidt](/wiki/About_Schmidt "About Schmidt")* (2002).
* The 2012 young adult novel *[Eleanor & Park](/wiki/Eleanor_%26_Park "Eleanor & Park")* by Omahan, [Rainbow Rowell](/wiki/Rainbow_Rowell "Rainbow Rowell"), takes place in Omaha.
* In [Better Call Saul](/wiki/Better_Call_Saul "Better Call Saul"), Omaha is the city that [Saul Goodman](/wiki/Saul_Goodman "Saul Goodman") is relocated to by [Ed Galbraith](/wiki/Ed_Galbraith "Ed Galbraith") after the events of [Breaking Bad](/wiki/Breaking_Bad "Breaking Bad"). There, he works and lives incognito as a fugitive under his alias "Gene Takavic"
